AIC-2101 Lab Guide

Spec-Driven Development with OpenSpec

Foundations & First Cycle

Lab 1

  • Set up and run the BeanBotics starter application
  • Initialize OpenSpec in an existing project and configure project context
  • Use an AI coding agent to explore a codebase and identify missing functionality
  • Complete a full propose-apply-archive cycle using OpenSpec’s core workflow
  • Practice context hygiene by clearing context between exploration and specification work

1.1. Prerequisites

Before starting this lab, ensure you have the following installed:

  • Python 3.10+ with pip

  • Node.js 18+ with npm (for OpenSpec CLI)

  • VS Code (or your preferred editor) with one of the seven supported AI coding agents installed and signed in (see Command syntax below for the list)

  • A terminal (the VS Code integrated terminal works well)

 Tip

If you have not completed the setup guide, do so before proceeding. The setup guide covers installing all prerequisites.

 Note

Command syntax depends on your agent

Throughout this course, when prose says "invoke the propose command," substitute the command name into the pattern for your agent:

Agent Example: propose

Claude Code

/opsx:propose

Cursor, Windsurf, GitHub Copilot, Google Antigravity

/opsx-propose

Amazon Q, AWS Kiro

@opsx-propose

Apply the same pattern to every other command (apply, archive, continue, …​).

 Note

If you use an agent outside these seven, check openspec/AGENTS.md for the exact syntax. Some agents use openspec- instead of opsx- as the command prefix.

1.2. Introduction

In this lab, you will experience the full Spec-Driven Development workflow for the first time. You will start by running a small web application called BeanBotics: an AI-themed coffee ordering system. After exploring the application and understanding what it does, you will use OpenSpec to propose, implement, and archive your first spec-driven feature.

The feature you will build is drink size selection. Right now, every order is placed as a medium. The menu data already includes pricing for small, medium, and large sizes, but the user interface does not let customers choose. By the end of this lab, customers will be able to select their preferred size before ordering.

This is deliberately a small change. The goal is not the feature itself. It is learning the workflow. You will use this same propose-apply-archive cycle throughout the course for progressively larger changes.

1.3. Exercise 1: Set Up the BeanBotics Starter App

In this exercise, you will open the lab’s BeanBotics folder in VS Code, create a virtual environment, install dependencies, and verify the app runs.

  1. Open the lab’s BeanBotics folder in VS Code. The course materials include a starter application at ~/LabFiles/foundations-first-cycle/beanbotics/. Open that folder as its own VS Code workspace:

    code ~/LabFiles/foundations-first-cycle/beanbotics

     Important

    For the rest of this lab, all work happens in this VS Code window with beanbotics as the project root. Your AI agent and the OpenSpec commands only work when the editor is opened to the directory where OpenSpec is initialized. If you have other editor windows open for other projects, that is fine. Just make sure your lab work happens in this window.

  2. Set up the Python environment. Open a terminal inside the VS Code window (Ctrl+`) and run:

    uv venv
uv pip install -r requirements.txt

  3. Run the application.

    uv run uvicorn backend.app:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000

    This starts a FastAPI server on http://localhost:8000.

  4. Verify the UI. Open http://localhost:8000 in your browser. Your UI should look like this:

    Notice the Order (Medium) buttons. Every drink can only be ordered in one size. You will change this later in the lab.

  5. Explore the UI. Open http://localhost:8000 in your browser. You should see:

    • A menu section with five AI-themed coffee drinks displayed as cards

    • Each card shows the drink name, description, price, and an Order (Medium) button

    • An order queue section that starts empty

      Try placing a few orders and cancelling one. Notice that:

    • Every order is placed as medium: there is no way to choose a size

    • The price shown is the medium price only

    • The order queue updates after each action

      Leave the server running in the terminal. You will use it throughout the lab; if it stops at any point, restart it with uv run uvicorn backend.app:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000.

       Note

      If port 8000 is already in use, change the --port argument in the uv run uvicorn …​ command above, or stop whatever is using that port.

1.4. Exercise 2: Pick Your Agent

You will work with one AI coding agent throughout this course. Pick one now and stay with it. Switching mid-course leaves half-configured projects behind. The seven supported agents fall into three invocation patterns; pick the tab below that matches the agent you intend to use.

Slash-colon (Claude Code)

  1. Install: the Claude Code extension in VS Code (or use the Claude Code CLI)

  2. Sign in: Ctrl+Shift+P → Claude Code: Sign In

  3. Verify the chat panel opens: Ctrl+Esc in VS Code

  4. Commands look like /opsx:propose

Slash-hyphen (Cursor, Windsurf, GitHub Copilot, Google Antigravity)

  1. Install your agent:

    • Cursor and Windsurf: download the standalone editor from cursor.com / windsurf.com

    • GitHub Copilot: install the GitHub Copilot Chat extension in VS Code

    • Google Antigravity: download the Antigravity IDE from antigravity.google

  2. Sign in: Cursor and Windsurf use their own accounts; Copilot uses your GitHub account; Antigravity uses your Google account

  3. Verify the chat panel opens:

    • Cursor and Windsurf: Ctrl+L

    • GitHub Copilot: Ctrl+Alt+I

    • Antigravity: the chat panel is in the IDE sidebar

  4. Commands look like /opsx-propose

@-mention (Amazon Q, AWS Kiro)

  1. Install your agent:

    • Amazon Q Developer: install the Amazon Q extension in VS Code

    • AWS Kiro: download the Kiro IDE from kiro.dev

  2. Sign in: both use your AWS Builder ID (or IAM Identity Center for enterprise users)

  3. Verify the chat panel opens:

    • Amazon Q: click the Q icon in the VS Code activity bar

    • AWS Kiro: the chat panel is open by default in the Kiro IDE

  4. Commands look like @opsx-propose (not slash-prefixed)

 Note

From here on, when this lab says "invoke the propose command," use the pattern for your agent from the Command syntax block at the top of this lab.

1.5. Exercise 3: Initialize OpenSpec

In this exercise, you will verify the OpenSpec CLI is available, initialize it in the BeanBotics project, and examine what it creates.

  1. Verify OpenSpec is installed. OpenSpec was installed on the lab VM during course setup. In a new terminal (keep the server running in the first one), confirm it is available:

    openspec --version

    You should see 1.2.0. If the command is not found or the version differs, the lab VM setup did not complete successfully. Alert your instructor before proceeding.

  2. Initialize OpenSpec in the project. In the VS Code terminal (which is already open inside beanbotics/), run:

    openspec init

    When prompted, select the AI tool you chose in Exercise 2. The core profile is used by default. You do not need to select it.

     Important

    After openspec init completes, reload the VS Code window so your AI agent picks up the newly-installed OpenSpec commands. Open the command palette with Ctrl+Shift+P, type Developer: Reload Window, and select it.

    The reload stops the BeanBotics server because it runs inside the integrated terminal. After the reload, open a new terminal with Ctrl+` and restart the server:

    uv run uvicorn backend.app:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000

    The OpenSpec commands only work when your editor is open to the directory where you ran openspec init.

  3. Examine the generated structure. After initialization, explore what OpenSpec created. In a terminal (not your agent’s chat panel), run:

    ls -la openspec/

    You should see:

    • openspec/. The specification directory with specs/, changes/, and optional config files

    • openspec/specs/. Currently empty. This is where the source-of-truth specifications will live after your first archive

    • openspec/changes/. Currently empty. This is where active work will be tracked

      OpenSpec also wrote command files into your agent’s own config directory. List that directory to see them. Which directory depends on the agent you selected:

      Table 1. How agents store their context
      Agent Command directory Invocation

      Claude Code

      .claude/commands/opsx/

      /opsx:propose

      Cursor

      .cursor/commands/

      /opsx-propose

      Windsurf

      .windsurf/workflows/

      /opsx-propose

      GitHub Copilot

      .github/prompts/

      /opsx-propose

      Google Antigravity

      .agent/workflows/

      /opsx-propose

      Amazon Q

      .amazonq/prompts/

      @opsx-propose

      AWS Kiro

      .kiro/prompts/

      @opsx-propose

      Your agent reads its own directory; the others are ignored. The course’s checkpoint folders ship all seven trees pre-generated, so you can switch agents between labs without re-running openspec init.

      Notice there are now two sources giving context to your coding agent:

    • AGENTS.md: already in the project. You (the developer) write and maintain this. It tells your agent about the project: tech stack, structure, conventions. AGENTS.md is an emerging cross-agent convention that most listed agents read directly; a few (like Claude Code) also read a tool-specific file such as CLAUDE.md, but AGENTS.md is the agent-neutral source of truth in this course.

    • Agent-specific commands: generated by OpenSpec in the directory above (e.g., .claude/commands/opsx/ for Claude Code, .cursor/commands/ for Cursor). These tell your agent how to work with the OpenSpec framework: available commands, where specs live, workflow conventions.

      The key distinction: AGENTS.md is yours to edit. The agent-specific command files are managed by OpenSpec. Do not edit them by hand. If you need to update them, run openspec update.

  4. Configure the project context. openspec init created openspec/config.yaml for you. Open it in VS Code and replace its contents with:

    schema: spec-driven

context: |
  BeanBotics is an AI-themed coffee ordering web application.
  Tech stack: Python 3.10+, FastAPI, vanilla HTML/CSS/JavaScript frontend.
  Backend: FastAPI app in backend/ with service layer pattern.
  Frontend: Single-page app in frontend/ using fetch API for all server calls.
  Data: Menu items loaded from backend/data/menu.json. Orders stored in memory.
  No database, no authentication, no payment processing.
  API endpoints: GET /api/menu, POST /api/orders, GET /api/orders, DELETE /api/orders/{id}.

    This context is injected into every artifact that OpenSpec generates, giving the AI agent accurate information about the project without needing to re-discover it each time.

     Tip

    Keep config.yaml context focused on facts the AI cannot easily infer from the code. Tech stack, architectural patterns, and non-obvious constraints are good. Generic best practices are not. The AI already knows those.

1.6. Exercise 4: Explore the Codebase with Your Agent

In this exercise, you will use your AI coding agent conversationally to understand the BeanBotics codebase before proposing any changes. This mirrors the realistic developer workflow: investigate first, then plan.

  1. Open your agent’s chat panel. Open the chat panel for the agent you set up in Exercise 2. The exact way to do this depends on the agent. Consult your agent’s documentation if you are unsure. Most agents offer either a sidebar icon or a keyboard shortcut.

  2. Ask your agent to explore. Ask your agent to examine the project. Here are some good exploratory prompts:

    What does this project do? Walk me through the main files and architecture.
    Look at the menu data in backend/data/menu.json. What drink sizes are available, and how does the frontend currently handle size selection?
    What would need to change to let customers choose between small, medium, and large sizes when ordering?

  3. Understand the gap. Through your exploration, you should discover:

    • The backend already supports sizes. The OrderRequest model accepts a size field, and the menu.json file has pricing for small, medium, and large

    • The frontend hardcodes medium. The placeOrder() function in script.js always sends size: "medium", and the order button says "Order (Medium)"

    • The price display only shows the medium price. The menu cards show "From $X.XX" using only the medium price

      This means the feature is primarily a frontend change: the backend is already ready.

  4. Clear your context. This is your first context hygiene practice. You have been exploring conversationally. Asking questions, reading code, building understanding. That exploration context is useful for you, but it will clutter the AI’s context window when you move to specification work.

    Clear the context now using your agent’s clear-context mechanism. Most agents offer either a slash command (such as /clear or /new), a "new chat" or "new session" button in the chat panel, or both. Consult your agent’s documentation if you cannot find it.

    This gives you a fresh context window for the next exercise. The specifications you create will be cleaner because the AI agent is not carrying forward noise from the exploration conversation.

     Note

    Your understanding persists in your head. The specifications persist in files. Clearing the AI’s context does not lose anything important. It just removes conversational noise.

1.7. Exercise 5: Your First Propose-Apply-Archive Cycle

In this exercise, you will use the core OpenSpec workflow to propose, implement, and archive your first spec-driven feature: drink size selection.

  1. Propose the feature. In your agent’s chat panel (with the fresh context from the previous step), invoke the propose command (see Command syntax above) with the change name add-drink-size-selection and this intent text:

    Add a size selector (small, medium, large) to the order form so
customers can choose a size before placing an order. The selected
size should drive the displayed price and be sent with the order.

Out of scope: drink customizations (milk, flavor, extra shots),
menu changes, new backend endpoints, and any UI changes beyond
the size selector and its price display.

    The first argument add-drink-size-selection is the change name. It becomes a directory name under openspec/changes/, so use a descriptive slug: lowercase, hyphens instead of spaces, no special characters. Think of it like a git branch name. It should read clearly in the file system and in git status.

    Everything after the slug is intent: a short description of what you want the feature to do, followed by explicit out-of-scope items. Without this, the agent infers intent from the codebase alone, and the codebase hints at more features than this one change should deliver. Feeding intent and scope boundaries up front keeps the proposal focused on what you asked for.

    OpenSpec will create a change folder and generate four artifacts. Watch the output as your agent works through the generation.

  2. Examine the generated artifacts. After the proposal completes, look at what was created. In your terminal, run:

    ls openspec/changes/add-drink-size-selection/

    You should see:

    • proposal.md. Why this change is being made, what is in scope, what is out of scope, and any risks

    • specs/. Delta specifications describing what changes in terms of ADDED, MODIFIED, and REMOVED requirements

    • design.md. Technical approach: what components change, how, and why

    • tasks.md. An ordered checklist of implementation steps

      Open each file and read through it. Ask yourself:

    • proposal.md: Does the scope match what you intended? Is anything included that should not be?

    • specs/: Are the scenarios concrete? Do they describe behavior (what the user sees) rather than implementation (how the code works)?

    • design.md: Does the technical approach make sense given what you learned during exploration?

    • tasks.md: Are the tasks small enough to verify individually?

       Important

      You do not need to edit anything in this first cycle. The goal is to see what OpenSpec generates and develop an eye for evaluating artifacts. In the next lab, you will practice reviewing and refining these artifacts.

  3. Apply the change. Now tell the AI agent to implement the feature based on the specifications. In your agent’s chat panel, invoke the apply command (see Command syntax above).

    Watch as your agent works through the tasks in tasks.md. It reads the specifications, design, and task checklist, then implements each step.

  4. Verify the feature. Once the apply step completes, check the running application in your browser. Refresh the page at http://localhost:8000.

    Your UI should now look something like this:

    You should see:

    • Each menu card now shows size options (small, medium, large) with their respective prices

    • Customers can select a size before ordering

    • The order queue shows the selected size in the order details

    • Ordering still works correctly. Place an order with a non-medium size and verify the price is correct

      If the server needs restarting after the code changes, stop it with Ctrl+C and run uv run uvicorn backend.app:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 again.

       Warning

      If something does not work correctly, do not fix it manually. This is a learning opportunity. Note what went wrong. You will learn debugging strategies in a later lab.

  5. Archive the change. Once you have verified the feature works, archive it. In your agent’s chat panel, invoke the archive command (see Command syntax above).

    This does two things:

    1. Merges delta specs into the main specifications. Check openspec/specs/: it now contains specification files describing the system’s current behavior, including the size selection feature you just added

    2. Moves the change to the archive. Check openspec/changes/archive/: the change folder is preserved with a timestamp for audit purposes

  6. Review the results. Examine the final state. In your terminal, run:

    # The source of truth -- now describes the current system
ls openspec/specs/

# The archive -- your completed change with timestamp
ls openspec/changes/archive/

# The active changes directory -- should be empty
ls openspec/changes/

    You have just completed a full propose-apply-archive cycle. The specifications describe what the system does. The archive records what changed and why. The code implements the behavior.

1.8. Reflection Questions

Take a few minutes to consider the following questions. These are not graded. They are meant to deepen your understanding.

  1. How did the specifications guide implementation? Think about what the AI agent did during the apply step. Did it follow the tasks? Did the specs constrain what it built?

  2. What would have happened without specs? If you had simply prompted your agent with "add size selection to the ordering UI," how might the result have differed? Would it have made different scope decisions?

  3. What is in openspec/specs/ now? Read through the generated specification file. Does it accurately describe the current system behavior? Is there anything missing or inaccurate?

  4. When would this workflow be overkill? Think about the size of this feature. Was the propose-apply-archive overhead worth it for drink size selection? For what size of change does the overhead start paying off?

  5. How did context clearing help? Did you notice a difference in the quality of the proposed artifacts after clearing the exploration context?

1.9. Common Issues

"OpenSpec commands not appearing in my agent"

Reload your editor after running openspec init. Most agents load their command files when the editor (or the agent extension) starts; until it reloads, the new commands will not appear. In VS Code-based agents, use Ctrl+Shift+P → Developer: Reload Window. Standalone-IDE agents (Cursor, Windsurf, Kiro) typically pick up new commands on restart.

"Server not starting. Port already in use"

Another process is using port 8000. The most common cause is a previous lab’s server still running. Find the terminal where uv run uvicorn …​ is running and press Ctrl+C. If you cannot locate it, restart your terminal session, or run uvicorn with a different --port argument.

"`openspec init` fails with permission error"

You may need to install the OpenSpec npm package with elevated permissions, or configure npm to use a different global directory. See the setup guide for details.

"Apply step produces errors or incomplete code"

This is expected in early learning. Common causes:

  • The AI agent’s context window is cluttered. Try clearing context and invoking the apply command again

  • The specs are ambiguous. Review the spec files for unclear language

  • A task is too broad. The AI tried to do too much in one step

Do not fix code manually in this lab. Note what happened and move on. Debugging strategies are covered in a later lab.

"Archive fails with validation error"

Run openspec validate to check for structural issues. The most common cause is incomplete tasks in tasks.md: all tasks should be checked off before archiving.

 Tip

Your work in ~/LabFiles/foundations-first-cycle/beanbotics/ is preserved after the lab. If you want to restart the lab from the clean starting state, re-extract the course materials ZIP (or copy the pristine folder from a fresh extract).

1.10. Challenge Exercise (Optional)

If you finish early, try this additional exercise.

1.10.1. Propose a Second Feature

Think of another small feature that would improve BeanBotics. Some ideas:

  • Display the order timestamp in the order queue

  • Add a "popular" badge to drinks with the highest price tier

  • Show a confirmation message after placing an order

Run through the full cycle again: propose, review the artifacts, apply, verify, archive. This time, pay closer attention to the quality of the generated specs. Are the scenarios concrete and testable?

 Note

Whatever you build here will be part of your project state, but it will not carry forward to the next lab. The next lab starts from a known checkpoint state.

1.11. Context Window Management Tips

These tips go beyond the basics covered in the lecture. You will develop your own feel for context management as you work through the course, but here are two common questions.

1.11.1. How Do I Know When to Clear My Context Window?

There is no hard rule. Watch for these signals:

  • Quality drops. The agent starts giving vague, repetitive, or off-target responses. It may "forget" things you told it earlier in the conversation or produce code that contradicts earlier decisions.

  • The conversation is long. If you have been working in the same session for a while. Especially with lots of exploration, debugging, and back-and-forth. The context window is filling up with noise that dilutes the signal.

  • You are switching tasks. Moving from exploration to specification work, or from one feature to another, is a natural breakpoint. Old context rarely helps and often hurts.

Most agents expose some form of session usage indicator. A token count, a context-fullness meter, or a /cost-style command. Find your agent’s equivalent and check it periodically; the cache or context read size gives you a rough sense of how full the window is. Your agent cannot check this on its own. You need to look.

 Tip

When in doubt, clear. The cost of a fresh start is low (a few seconds). The cost of a cluttered context is subtle and cumulative. You may not notice the quality degradation until you compare the output to what a fresh session produces.

1.11.2. How Do I Transition Without Losing Important Context?

Before clearing your context or starting a new session, ask the agent to write a handoff document: a short summary of what it learned, what decisions were made, and what is still open. Save this to a file in your project.

When you start the new session, point the agent at that file. It picks up where the previous session left off, but with a clean context window free of conversational noise.

Write a handoff document summarizing what we've learned, decisions made,
and open questions. Save it to docs/handoff.md.

This pattern works for any context transition: clearing within a session, switching between sessions, or handing off to a teammate. The specifications themselves serve this purpose for what the system does, but a handoff document captures the working context: what was tried, what was ruled out, and why.

Exploring, Proposing & Specifying

Lab 2

  • Use the explore command to investigate an existing codebase and discover missing functionality
  • Practice context hygiene by clearing context between exploration and specification work
  • Propose a feature informed by exploration findings using the propose command
  • Review and refine AI-generated artifacts: proposal, specs, design, and tasks
  • Complete a full explore-propose-apply-archive cycle with a refined specification

2.1. Prerequisites

Before starting this lab, ensure you have:

  • Completed the Foundations & First Cycle lab (or have the checkpoint files)

  • Python 3.10+ with pip

  • Node.js 18+ with npm

  • VS Code (or your preferred editor) with one of the seven supported AI coding agents installed and signed in (see the Command syntax block below)

  • OpenSpec CLI installed (openspec --version to verify)

 Note

Command syntax depends on your agent

Throughout this course, when prose says "invoke the propose command," substitute the command name into the pattern for your agent:

Agent Example: propose

Claude Code

/opsx:propose

Cursor, Windsurf, GitHub Copilot, Google Antigravity

/opsx-propose

Amazon Q, AWS Kiro

@opsx-propose

Apply the same pattern to every other command (apply, archive, continue, …​).

2.2. Introduction

In the previous lab, the feature scope was handed to you: "add drink size selection." You followed the propose-apply-archive workflow, and it worked. In real development, however, features rarely arrive pre-scoped. More often, you start with a vague idea: "something is missing" or "this could be better."

This lab teaches the explore-first pattern. You will investigate the BeanBotics codebase, discover existing infrastructure that is not being used, and use that understanding to write a well-scoped proposal. Then (this is the core exercise) you will review and refine the AI-generated artifacts before applying them.

The feature you will build is an order status lifecycle with a visual queue board. Right now, orders are either "pending" or "cancelled." But the data model already defines a richer set of statuses that no one has wired up yet. You will discover this during exploration, propose activating it, refine the specs, and implement the result.

2.3. Exercise 1: Open This Lab’s BeanBotics Folder

The starting state for this lab is already in place. This is BeanBotics with size selection, OpenSpec initialized, and one archived change. The completed state from a prior lab.

  1. Open this lab’s BeanBotics folder in VS Code. The course materials include a starter application at ~/LabFiles/exploring-proposing-specifying/beanbotics/. Open that folder as its own VS Code workspace:

    code ~/LabFiles/exploring-proposing-specifying/beanbotics

    If VS Code is already open on a different folder, open a new window (File → New Window) and open this folder in the new window, or close the current window and reopen with the command above.

     Important

    For the rest of this lab, all work happens in this editor window with beanbotics as the project root. Your agent and its OpenSpec commands only work when the editor is opened to the directory where OpenSpec is initialized. The commands are already available. This lab’s folder ships agent-config directories for all seven supported agents with the OpenSpec commands pre-installed.

  2. Set up the Python environment. Open a terminal inside the VS Code window (Ctrl+`) and run:

    uv venv
uv pip install -r requirements.txt

  3. Clean up from any prior session. If port 8000 is in use from a previous lab session, stop that server first. The most common cause is the previous lab’s uv run uvicorn …​ still running in another terminal. Find that terminal and press Ctrl+C. If you cannot locate the terminal, restart your terminal session.

  4. Run the app.

    uv run uvicorn backend.app:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000

    Verify the app is running at http://localhost:8000. You should see the menu with size selection from a prior lab.

2.4. Exercise 2: Explore the Order System

In the previous lab, you explored with freeform prompts. This time, you will use the explore command. OpenSpec’s dedicated exploration mode. Explore mode puts the AI agent into thinking-partner mode: it investigates, asks clarifying questions, and helps you understand the codebase without creating any artifacts.

  1. Start exploration. Open your agent’s chat panel and invoke the explore command (substitute the syntax for your agent from the Command syntax block above). When the agent enters explore mode, ask it to investigate the order system:

    Look at the Order model in the backend. What status values does it define?
How are those statuses actually used in the codebase? Is anything defined
but not implemented?

  2. Follow the trail. The agent should discover something interesting. The Order model in backend/models.py defines a status field with a comment listing five values:

    • pending

    • preparing

    • ready

    • completed

    • cancelled

      But if you look at the actual code in backend/services/orders.py:

    • place_order() always creates orders with status "pending"

    • cancel_order() changes status to "cancelled"

    • No code anywhere transitions orders through preparing, ready, or completed

      This is the gap: there is infrastructure designed for a full order lifecycle, but only two of the five statuses are used. Three statuses exist in the model but have no code path to reach them.

  3. Think through the solution. Continue the exploration by asking the agent to think through what a full lifecycle would look like:

    If we wanted to activate the full order lifecycle -- pending through
preparing, ready, and completed -- what would need to change? Think
about both the backend (API, validation) and the frontend (how would
the order queue display different statuses?).

    The agent will analyze the codebase and present options. Because AI responses are non-deterministic, your agent’s analysis may be organized differently from your instructor’s or your neighbor’s. Different labels, different option numbering, different emphasis. That is expected. The underlying design questions are the same regardless of how the agent frames them.

    As you read the agent’s response, watch for these decision points:

    • Who triggers status transitions? The agent may suggest a separate staff/barista view, automatic timers, or inline "advance" buttons on each order. These are different approaches to the same question: how does an order move from pending to preparing to ready to completed?

    • When can an order be cancelled? Currently only pending orders can be cancelled. Should that extend to orders that are already being prepared?

    • What happens to completed orders? Do they disappear from the list? Stay visible in a separate group? Accumulate indefinitely?

    • API design: One endpoint for all transitions, or separate endpoints per action? How are invalid transitions rejected?

      You do not need to answer these questions during exploration. Just make sure you understand them. You will make your decisions in the next step.

  4. Draft a proposal summary. You now have a clear understanding of the scope and the design decisions involved. Before clearing context, capture that understanding while the agent still has it. And bake in your decisions so the draft is not open-ended.

    Use a prompt like this, with your decisions included:

    Good analysis. Let's capture this as a draft proposal summary.
Here are my decisions on the open questions:

- Advance buttons inline on each order in the existing UI (no separate
  staff view, no automatic timers)
- Cancel from pending only (keep the current restriction)
- Completed orders stay visible in a "Completed" group on the queue board
- Single endpoint for all status transitions with a transition map
  that validates and rejects invalid moves

Write a draft proposal summary with these decisions baked in.
Include: the problem, proposed solution, scope, out of scope, and risks.
Save it to docs/explore-status-lifecycle.md.

     Note

    The decisions above keep the feature focused and achievable within lab time. Inline advance buttons avoid the complexity of a separate staff view or background timers. Restricting cancellation to pending orders keeps the transition logic simple. Showing completed orders in a group makes the full lifecycle visible.

    The agent will produce a concise summary incorporating your decisions. Review it quickly and adjust anything that does not match your intent.

    This draft is a bridge document. It captures the agent’s findings and your decisions in a durable file that survives context clearing. Without it, the next session starts cold and the propose step has to rediscover everything, or worse, make different decisions than you intended.

  5. Clear your context. With the draft saved to a file, reset your agent’s context so the next exercise starts clean. In Claude Code, type /clear; in other agents, open a new chat (or use the equivalent context-reset action).

     Note

    This is the context hygiene practice from the lecture. You explored in one context and will propose in a fresh one. Your understanding persists in the draft file and in your head. The conversation noise gets left behind.

2.5. Exercise 3: Propose the Order Status Lifecycle

With a fresh context window and a clear understanding of the scope, you are ready to propose.

  1. Run the proposal. Point the agent at your draft before proposing. This gives it the findings from exploration without the conversation noise. Ask your agent to read docs/explore-status-lifecycle.md for context, then invoke the propose command (using your agent’s syntax) with the change name add-order-status-lifecycle.

    Watch as the AI agent generates the four artifacts. Because it has both the draft summary and openspec/config.yaml, it produces artifacts informed by your exploration without needing to rediscover anything.

  2. Quick orientation. Before diving into a detailed review, get a quick sense of what was generated. In your terminal:

    ls openspec/changes/add-order-status-lifecycle/

    You should see:

    • proposal.md

    • specs/

    • design.md

    • tasks.md

      In the previous lab, you glanced at these artifacts and moved on. This time, you will read each one carefully and make improvements.

2.6. Exercise 4: Review and Refine the Artifacts

This is the core exercise of this lab. The AI-generated artifacts are a starting point, not a final product. Your job is to evaluate each artifact against the criteria from the lecture and make edits where needed.

Open each file in VS Code and review it using the guidance below. Edit the files directly: these are plain Markdown files, and the AI agent will use whatever you write when it implements the change.

  1. Review the proposal. Open openspec/changes/add-order-status-lifecycle/proposal.md and ask:

    • Does the scope match your intent? You want forward-only status transitions and a visual status board. Did the AI add anything beyond that. Order history, analytics, notifications, time estimates? If so, add those to the "out of scope" section.

    • Is the problem statement clear? It should say something like: the order model defines five statuses but only two are used. The application has no way to advance orders through the lifecycle.

    • Are risks identified? For example: the current UI shows a flat order list; changing it to a grouped display changes the visual structure significantly.

      Make edits as needed. If the proposal includes features you did not intend, remove them and add them to "out of scope." If the problem statement is vague, sharpen it.

  2. Review the specifications. Open the spec files in openspec/changes/add-order-status-lifecycle/specs/. This is the most important artifact to review. Look for:

    Are requirements using RFC 2119 keywords?

    If you see a requirement like "the system updates the order status," change it to use precise language: "the system MUST update the order status" or "the system SHOULD display a confirmation."

    Are scenarios concrete?

    Compare:

    # Weak -- vague outcome
Given an order exists
When the status is advanced
Then the order should update correctly

# Strong -- concrete values and observable behavior
Given an order with status "pending"
When the barista advances the order
Then the order status changes to "preparing"
  and the order queue displays the order under the "Preparing" group

    If you find vague scenarios, rewrite them with concrete values.

    Is there a scenario for invalid transitions?

    The AI may have generated only happy-path scenarios. Check whether these edge cases are covered:

    • Advancing a cancelled order (should be rejected)

    • Advancing a completed order (should be rejected. It is already terminal)

    • Cancelling an order that is no longer pending (should be rejected)

      If any of these are missing, add them. This is where your understanding from the exploration step pays off. An example to add if missing:

      #### Scenario: Cannot advance a cancelled order
- **Given** an order with status "cancelled"
- **When** a request is made to advance the order
- **Then** the system rejects the request
  and the order status remains "cancelled"

      Are there implementation details where behavioral requirements belong?

      More detail is better, but the detail should describe what the system does, not how the code is structured. If you see references to specific CSS classes, variable names, or function signatures, replace them with behavioral language. For example:

    • Implementation detail: "Use a status_transitions dictionary to validate transitions"

    • Behavioral requirement: "The system MUST enforce forward-only transitions and reject invalid transition requests"

      Both are specific. The difference: the behavioral version still holds if someone refactors the internals. The implementation version breaks.

      Here is a real example the author encountered when testing this lab. The AI generated this requirement for status badges:

      Badge styles SHALL be implemented as CSS classes:
`.status-pending`, `.status-preparing`, `.status-ready`,
`.status-completed`, `.status-cancelled`.

      This dictates how the badge is built (specific CSS class names) rather than what the user sees. Replace it with a behavioral requirement:

      Each status MUST be visually distinct. The badge color
MUST clearly differentiate pending, preparing, ready,
completed, and cancelled orders.

      The AI is free to use CSS classes, inline styles, data attributes, or any other technique. The spec only cares that the user can tell statuses apart at a glance. Add as many edge cases and constraints as you want. Just keep them at the behavior level.

  3. Review the design. Open openspec/changes/add-order-status-lifecycle/design.md and check:

    • Does the technical approach fit the existing codebase? The backend uses a service layer pattern (routes in app.py delegate to service classes). The design should follow this pattern.

    • Is the API design sensible? Check that the design includes an endpoint for status transitions and validation of transition rules.

    • Does the frontend approach make sense? The existing frontend uses vanilla JavaScript with fetch() calls. The design should not introduce new frameworks or libraries.

      Notice that design.md contains the kind of detail you removed from the specs. This is intentional. Specs and design have different jobs:

    • Specs describe what the system does: "The system MUST enforce forward-only transitions and reject invalid transition requests."

    • Design describes how to build it: "Define a transition map as a dictionary in models.py alongside the enum. Declarative, co-located with the data model, testable in isolation."

      Same feature, different layers. If someone replaced the dictionary with a state machine library, every scenario in the spec would still pass. The design doc is where implementation choices like data structures, file placement, API verb selection, and architectural patterns belong. This is the complement to the rule in Step 2: strip implementation details from specs, but expect to find them in the design.

  4. Review the tasks. Open openspec/changes/add-order-status-lifecycle/tasks.md and check:

    • Are tasks small and independently verifiable? A task like "implement the order status feature" is too broad. It should be broken into steps: add the status transition logic, add the API endpoint, update the frontend display, add the advance action.

    • Are dependencies ordered correctly? Backend changes should come before frontend changes that depend on new API endpoints.

    • Is each task clear enough that you could verify it without reading the code? For example: "After this task, PUT /api/orders/1/status with {"status": "preparing"} returns 200 for a pending order and 400 for a cancelled order."

       Tip

      Reviewing and editing artifacts is a skill that develops over the course. You are not expected to catch everything on the first pass. The goal is to build the habit of reading critically rather than accepting defaults.

2.7. Exercise 5: Apply, Verify, and Archive

  1. Apply the refined specs. With your refined artifacts in place, invoke the apply command (using your agent’s syntax) to tell the AI agent to implement the change.

    The agent reads your edited specs, design, and tasks. Watch how it works through the task checklist. Because you refined the specs, the implementation should align more closely with your intent than if you had accepted the defaults.

  2. Verify the feature. Refresh the application in your browser at http://localhost:8000. Place a few orders, advance some through the lifecycle, and test the new functionality. Your UI should look something like this:

    What to verify:

    • Status-grouped display: The order queue should organize orders by status rather than showing a flat list. Look for visual groupings like "Pending," "Preparing," "Ready," and "Completed" with color-coded badges or headers.

    • Advance action: Each non-terminal order should have a way to advance it to the next status. Click it and watch the order move from one group to the next.

    • Forward-only transitions: Try to advance a completed order. It should not be possible. Cancel a pending order, then try to advance it. It should be rejected.

    • Cancel restriction: Only pending orders should be cancellable. Orders that have moved to "preparing" or beyond should not have a cancel option.

      The server runs with --reload, so backend Python changes are picked up automatically. However, your browser caches frontend files (JavaScript, CSS). If the UI looks unchanged after apply, do a hard refresh to bypass the cache: Ctrl+Shift+R. If that does not help, stop the server with Ctrl+C and run uv run uvicorn backend.app:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 again.

       Warning

      If a behavioral requirement is not met. Wrong transition rules, missing validation, incorrect grouping. Think about whether the spec is unclear before asking the AI to fix code. Fix the spec first, then re-apply.

       Tip

      If the behavior is correct but something looks wrong: an ugly button, poor spacing, a color that does not fit the theme. Just tell the coding agent to fix it directly. You do not need OpenSpec for cosmetic fixes. The spec says "display an action to advance each non-terminal order." The AI chose a button and the button works, but it is the wrong shade of green? That is not a spec issue. Ask the agent: "Make the advance button match the style of the existing cancel button." This is the "when SDD is overkill" principle in practice: if every scenario in the spec still passes, the fix does not need a spec.

  3. Archive the change. Once the feature is working, invoke the archive command (using your agent’s syntax).

  4. Review the accumulated specs. In your terminal, examine the specifications after archiving:

    ls openspec/specs/

    You now have spec files covering two domain areas: drink size selection (from the previous lab) and order status lifecycle. The archive contains both completed changes with timestamps.

    ls openspec/changes/archive/

    These specs are becoming the source of truth for the BeanBotics system. If a new developer joined the project, they could read openspec/specs/ to understand what the system does without reading every line of code.

 Tip

Your work in ~/LabFiles/exploring-proposing-specifying/beanbotics/ is preserved after the lab. If you want to restart the lab from the clean starting state, re-extract the course materials ZIP (or copy the pristine folder from a fresh extract).

2.8. Reflection Questions

Take a few minutes to consider these questions.

  1. How did exploration change your proposal? Compare what you would have proposed without exploring to what you actually proposed after discovering the unused status values. Did exploration narrow or widen the scope?

  2. How useful was the draft proposal summary? Compare the draft you wrote at the end of exploration to the artifacts OpenSpec generated. Did the draft help the AI produce better artifacts? Was anything in the draft lost or misinterpreted?

  3. What did you change in the artifacts? Think about the edits you made during the review step. Which artifact needed the most work? What kinds of issues did you find?

  4. How did spec quality affect implementation? If you tightened a vague scenario or added an edge case, did the AI handle it correctly during apply? If you left something vague, did the AI make assumptions you did not expect?

  5. What would "accept all defaults" have produced? If you had skipped the review step entirely and gone straight from propose to apply, how might the result have differed?

  6. When is the review step worth the time? This lab’s feature was moderately complex. For a smaller change, would the review step still be worth it? Where is the threshold?

2.9. Common Issues

"Menu stuck on 'Loading menu…​' and never populates"

The browser tab is left over from a previous lab. It was open before the current server started, so the menu fetch is pending against a server that no longer exists. Do a hard refresh with Ctrl+Shift+R. If that does not fix it, confirm the URL is http://localhost:8000/ and that uv run uvicorn backend.app:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 is still running in the terminal.

"The explore command does not seem to do anything different"

Explore mode is conversational. It does not create files or artifacts. The agent investigates and discusses, but you will not see new files in openspec/changes/. That is intentional. Explore is for understanding; propose is for artifacts.

"The AI generated specs that are too implementation-specific"

This is common, especially with detailed codebases. The AI reads the code and mirrors its structure in the specs. When you see function names, CSS classes, or variable names in specs, replace them with behavioral descriptions. This is normal and expected. It is why the review step exists.

"I am not sure what to edit in the artifacts"

Start with the specs and look for scenarios. Ask: "Could I test this scenario without reading the code?" If the answer is no, the scenario needs more concrete values or clearer outcomes. You do not need to rewrite everything. Even one improved scenario is valuable practice.

"UI looks unchanged after apply"

The server picks up backend (Python) changes automatically via --reload, but your browser caches frontend files (JavaScript, CSS). Do a hard refresh with Ctrl+Shift+R. If that does not help, stop the server with Ctrl+C and restart with uv run uvicorn backend.app:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000.

"Apply produced errors or incorrect behavior"

Check the specs first. A common cause is conflicting requirements or missing edge cases. If the spec says orders "can be" cancelled but does not specify which statuses allow cancellation, the AI may make a different assumption than you intended. Fix the spec, clear context, and re-apply.

"The frontend looks different from what I expected"

The spec describes behavior (orders grouped by status, advance action available), not appearance (specific colors, layout, animations). Visual differences are acceptable as long as the behavior matches. If a behavioral requirement is not met, that is a spec issue.

"Archive fails with validation errors"

Run openspec validate to identify structural issues. The most common cause is unchecked tasks in tasks.md. Ensure all tasks are marked complete before archiving.

2.10. Challenge Exercise (Optional)

If you finish early, try one of these additional exercises.

2.10.1. Write a Scenario From Scratch

Think of an edge case that is not covered by the specs you just archived. For example:

  • What happens when multiple orders are advanced simultaneously?

  • What should the queue show when there are no orders in a particular status group?

  • Should completed orders disappear after a certain time or accumulate indefinitely?

Write a complete Given/When/Then scenario for your chosen edge case. Do not implement it. Just write the scenario and evaluate whether it is concrete enough for an AI agent to implement correctly.

2.10.2. Compare Spec Quality

Open the archived spec from the first lab (openspec/changes/archive/: the drink size selection change) and compare it to the order status lifecycle spec you just archived. Consider:

  • Which has more concrete scenarios?

  • Which better separates behavior from implementation?

  • How would you improve the first lab’s spec with what you know now?

 Note

Whatever you build in the challenge exercise is for learning purposes. It will not carry forward to the next lab, which starts from a known checkpoint state.

2.11. Context Hygiene Recap

This lab practiced the draft-and-clear pattern: explore, draft a proposal summary, clear context, then propose using the draft as input.

The draft proposal summary is the key step. It solves two problems at once:

  • It captures what the agent learned. Without a draft, clearing context discards the agent’s findings. The next session starts cold and the propose step has to rediscover everything, or worse, guesses.

  • It filters out noise. A two-paragraph summary preserves the signal (what to build, what to avoid) without the dead ends, corrections, and back-and-forth of an exploration conversation.

When is the draft-and-clear pattern worth it?

  • You explored an unfamiliar area of the codebase. The exploration was valuable but noisy.

  • The exploration involved dead ends or corrections. Noise that would confuse the propose step.

  • You want artifacts that stand on their own. The draft ensures spec quality is not diluted by conversation history.

You do not always need to clear context. If your exploration was brief and focused, and you are ready to propose in the same session, the overhead may not be worth it. Use judgment. Context hygiene is a skill, not a rigid rule.

Implementation Patterns & Debugging

Lab 3

  • Complete two propose-apply-archive cycles with different levels of review depth
  • Reference existing specifications when proposing a change that depends on prior work
  • Observe how the AI agent executes tasks during apply
  • Trace unexpected implementation behavior back to a gap in the specification
  • Apply the debugging strategy: identify the spec gap, add the missing requirement, re-apply

3.1. Prerequisites

Before starting this lab, ensure you have:

  • Completed the Exploring, Proposing & Specifying lab (or have the checkpoint files)

  • Python 3.10+ with pip

  • Node.js 18+ with npm

  • VS Code (or your preferred editor) with an AI coding agent installed and signed in

  • OpenSpec CLI installed (openspec --version to verify)

 Note

Command syntax depends on your agent

Throughout this course, when prose says "invoke the propose command," substitute the command name into the pattern for your agent:

Agent Example: propose

Claude Code

/opsx:propose

Cursor, Windsurf, GitHub Copilot, Google Antigravity

/opsx-propose

Amazon Q, AWS Kiro

@opsx-propose

Apply the same pattern to every other command (apply, archive, continue, …​).

3.2. Introduction

In the previous labs, you built one feature per lab. You learned the core workflow (propose, apply, archive), how to explore before proposing, and how to review and refine artifacts. Each feature stood on its own. Size selection and the order lifecycle were independent changes.

This lab changes that. You will build two features that depend on each other:

  1. Drink customizations: customers can add extras to their drinks: extra shot ($0.75), milk alternatives like oat, almond, or soy ($0.60), and whipped cream (+$0.50).

  2. Order confirmation and receipt view: before placing an order, customers see a confirmation step. After an order is completed, they can view a receipt with a line-item breakdown: base price, each customization, tax, and total.

The dependency: the receipt must show customization details and prices from the first feature. This is the first time a change you build depends on a change you just finished.

Along the way, you will practice two new skills:

  • Adjusting your review depth. Not every change needs the same scrutiny. Change 1 is well-scoped. A quick scan of the artifacts is enough. Change 2 has a dependency and a subtle spec ambiguity that requires closer attention.

  • Debugging spec ambiguity. When the implementation does not match your expectations, the root cause is often a gap in the spec, not a bug in the code. You will experience this firsthand and learn to trace the problem back to its source.

3.3. Exercise 1: Set Up Your Working Copy

This is the completed state from a previous lab. BeanBotics with size selection, the order status lifecycle, and two archived changes.

  1. Open this lab’s BeanBotics folder in VS Code. The starter state for this lab is at ~/LabFiles/implementation-patterns-debugging/beanbotics/. Open it as its own VS Code workspace:

    code ~/LabFiles/implementation-patterns-debugging/beanbotics

    If VS Code is already open on a different folder, open a new window (File → New Window) and open this folder in the new window, or close the current window and reopen with the command above.

     Important

    For the rest of this lab, all work happens in this VS Code window with beanbotics as the project root. Your agent’s OpenSpec commands are already available. The starter state includes the agent-config directories from the earlier labs.

  2. Set up the Python environment. Open a terminal inside the VS Code window (Ctrl+`) and run:

    uv venv
uv pip install -r requirements.txt

  3. Clean up from any previous lab session. If you still have a server running from an earlier lab, stop it now. Press Ctrl+C in the terminal where it is running. If you cannot locate that terminal, restart your terminal session to release the port.

  4. Run your app.

    uv run uvicorn backend.app:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000

    Verify the app is running at http://localhost:8000. You should see the menu with size selection and the status-grouped order queue from the earlier labs.

  5. Review your starting state. Before building new features, take a quick look at what you are starting with. In your terminal:

    ls openspec/specs/

    You should see three spec files from the two features built in previous labs: drink-size-selection, order-status-lifecycle, and order-status-ui. These are the behavioral contracts for the system as it exists now.

    ls openspec/changes/archive/

    Two archived changes with timestamps. The complete history of what has been built so far.

    This accumulated spec suite is important. In the next exercise, you will build on it, and in Exercise 3, you will reference it explicitly when proposing a dependent change.

3.4. Exercise 2: Drink Customizations. The Fast Cycle

This exercise is a complete propose-apply-archive cycle, but at a faster pace than previous labs. The feature is well-scoped and the scope is clear from the start. You do not need to explore first, and you do not need to do a deep artifact review.

The goal: practice adjusting your review depth to match the complexity of the change. Not every feature needs the same level of scrutiny. Drink customizations is a straightforward addition. Save your detailed review energy for Exercise 3.

  1. Propose drink customizations. Open your agent’s chat panel and invoke the propose command (see Command syntax above) with the change name add-drink-customizations and the following prompt body:

    Customers should be able to add optional extras when ordering any drink:
- Extra espresso shot: +$0.75
- Milk alternative (oat, almond, or soy): +$0.60
- Whipped cream: +$0.50

Customization options should appear on each menu card. The order total
should update dynamically as customizations are selected. The order queue
and status board should display any customizations on each order.

Out of scope: per-drink customization restrictions (all options available
for all drinks), customization inventory tracking, recurring/saved
customization preferences.

     Note

    This proposal includes specific prices, a clear list of options, scope boundaries, and explicit out-of-scope items. Because the intent is precise, the AI generates better artifacts with less correction needed.

  2. Quick scan of artifacts. Open the generated artifacts in VS Code. This time, do a quick scan rather than the deep review you practiced in the previous lab:

    • proposal.md: Does the scope match your intent? Did the AI add features you did not ask for? (Skim in under a minute.)

    • specs/: Are customization prices correct ($0.75, $0.60, $0.50)? Do the scenarios cover adding customizations to an order and seeing them in the queue? (Skim the scenarios for obvious gaps.)

    • design.md: Does the approach fit the existing codebase? (Quick check. Not a deep review.)

    • tasks.md: Are backend tasks before frontend tasks? (Glance at the ordering.)

      If anything is clearly wrong (wrong prices, scope creep, obviously missing scenarios), fix it. Otherwise, move on. The goal is to build the habit of matching review depth to risk. This change is well-scoped with clear requirements, so a light review is appropriate.

       Note

      During lab testing, the course author found that design.md contained several instances of implementation-level detail that belongs in specs or tasks, not design. For example, the AI generated a decisions section that named specific Python variables and type signatures: "Define CUSTOMIZATIONS: dict[str, float] in models.py mapping option IDs to prices." The design-level version of that decision is: "The set of options is small, universal across all drinks, and unlikely to change frequently. Defining them as a constant in the backend keeps them co-located with the data model." The principle: design documents capture decisions and rationale (why X over Y), not variable names, type signatures, or file paths. If you spot this in your artifacts, clean it up, but do not let it block you from moving forward. See the extra material at the end of this lab for more detail.

  3. Apply and observe. Before running apply, keep these questions in mind as you watch the agent work:

    • Does the agent work through tasks in the order listed in tasks.md?

    • When it hits something not covered by the spec, does it ask or assume?

    • Does it refer back to the specs or design, or does it just write code?

    • Does it check off tasks as it completes them?

      Now invoke the apply command.

      Watch the agent work. You do not need to intervene. Just observe. After it finishes, note your answers to the questions above. You will discuss these observations during the reflection at the end of the lab.

  4. Verify in the browser. Refresh the application at http://localhost:8000. If the UI looks unchanged, do a hard refresh: Ctrl+Shift+R.

    What to verify:

    • Customization options visible: Each menu card should show options for extra shot, milk alternative, and whipped cream.

    • Dynamic price updates: Selecting customizations should update the displayed order price. A Large Neural Network Latte ($6.50) with an extra shot ($0.75) should show $7.25.

    • Order placement with customizations: Place an order with one or more customizations. The order queue should show the customizations on the order card.

    • Customizations on the status board: Advance a customized order through the lifecycle. The customization details should remain visible at each status.

       Tip

      If the behavior is correct but something looks rough. Awkward layout, clashing colors, poor spacing. Just tell the agent directly: "Fix the customization layout to match the existing card style." No spec needed for cosmetic fixes.

  5. Archive. Invoke the archive command.

    Verify that the customization specs have been merged into openspec/specs/:

    ls openspec/specs/

    You should see new spec files covering the customization domain alongside the existing size selection and order lifecycle specs.

  6. Clear context. Clear your conversation context before starting the next change. Most agents support this through a "new chat" / "clear context" command or button. Consult your agent’s docs for the exact mechanism (for example, Claude Code uses /clear).

    Change 2 depends on Change 1, but you will handle that dependency explicitly in the next exercise. By pointing the agent at the archived specs, not by carrying forward conversation history.

3.5. Exercise 3: Order Confirmation & Receipt. Dependency Handling

This change depends on the customization feature you just built. Receipts must show each customization as a separate line item with its individual price. This is the first time you are proposing a change that explicitly depends on a prior change.

  1. Reference existing specs when proposing. After archiving Change 1, the customization specs were merged into openspec/specs/. The agent can see the code from Change 1 (it is in the codebase), but the specs capture behavioral contracts. Edge cases, constraints, and pricing rules. That the code alone does not communicate.

    Feed the specs as context when proposing. First, send the following message to your agent:

    Read the specs in openspec/specs/ for context on the current system,
especially the customization pricing model.

This change adds two things:
1. An order confirmation step: after selecting a drink and customizations,
   the customer sees a "Review Your Order" summary before confirming.
   The summary shows the drink name and size, each selected customization
   with its price, and the order total. The customer can confirm or go back.
2. A receipt view for completed orders: when an order reaches "completed"
   status, it has a receipt showing a line-item breakdown:
   - Base price (drink name, size, price)
   - Each customization as a separate line item with its price
   - Subtotal
   - Total including tax

Out of scope: printing receipts, emailing receipts, receipt history page,
tax configuration UI.

    Then invoke the propose command with the change name add-order-confirmation-receipt.

     Note

    Notice what the proposal does not say: the tax rate, whether tax is applied per item or on the subtotal, and how to round. The phrase "total including tax" is deliberately vague. You will investigate what the AI does with that ambiguity in Exercise 4.

  2. Review artifacts. Focus on the dependency. This time, review the artifacts with more care than Exercise 2. Pay attention to how the generated specs handle the dependency on customizations:

    In proposal.md:

    • Does it acknowledge the dependency on customizations?

    • Is the scope right? The confirmation step and receipt view are distinct features. Make sure neither one was dropped or over-expanded.

      In specs/:

    • Do the receipt scenarios reference customization line items? There should be a scenario showing a receipt with at least one customization.

    • Is the line-item structure specified? The receipt should break down: base price, customizations (each on its own line with price), subtotal, tax, total.

    • Is the tax calculation specified? Look for: the tax rate, what it applies to (subtotal), and how rounding works. Note what is present and what is missing: you will need this information in Exercise 4.

      In design.md:

    • Does the API design make sense? A receipt likely needs a new endpoint or an extension to the existing order endpoint.

    • Does the frontend approach fit the existing vanilla JS codebase?

      In tasks.md:

    • Are receipt-related tasks after the confirmation step? (The confirmation step is simpler and does not depend on pricing logic.)

      Edit any issues you find. In particular, if the tax rate or rounding strategy is not specified in the specs, leave it that way for now. You will use that gap as a debugging exercise in Exercise 4.

  3. Apply. Invoke the apply command.

    Watch the agent work through the tasks. Because the specs reference customization behavior from the archived specs, the agent should produce an implementation that integrates customization line items into the receipt.

  4. Verify the confirmation step. Refresh the application at http://localhost:8000 (hard refresh: Ctrl+Shift+R).

    • Select a drink and customizations. Click the order button.

    • Confirmation step appears. You should see a "Review Your Order" summary showing the drink, size, any customizations, and the total price.

    • Confirm or go back. Confirming should place the order. Going back should return to the menu without placing the order.

3.6. Exercise 4: Debugging Spec Ambiguity

Now for the debugging exercise. You will verify the receipt feature with a specific test order and check whether the implementation matches your expectations.

  1. Create a test order. Place a specific order so you can calculate the expected receipt values by hand:

    1. Select a Large Neural Network Latte ($6.50)

    2. Add an extra shot (+$0.75)

    3. Confirm and place the order

  2. Calculate the expected receipt. Before looking at the receipt, calculate what it should show:

    Large Neural Network Latte .......... $6.50
  Extra espresso shot ............... $0.75
                          Subtotal    $7.25
                        Tax (8.5%)    $0.62
                             Total    $7.87

     Note

    The tax calculation: $7.25 x 0.085 = $0.61625, rounded to the nearest cent = $0.62. Total: $7.25 + $0.62 = $7.87.

  3. Advance the order and check the receipt. Advance the test order through the lifecycle: pending → preparing → ready → completed. Once it reaches completed status, view the receipt.

    Compare the receipt to your hand calculation. Check:

    • Are the line items correct? Base price, customization, subtotal.

    • Is the tax rate 8.5%? If the spec did not specify a rate, the AI chose one. What did it choose?

    • Is the tax amount correct? Calculate it yourself and compare.

    • Does the total match? Even a one-cent difference matters. It reveals a spec gap.

  4. Diagnose any differences. If the receipt matches your calculation exactly, check whether the spec actually specified all the details. Open the spec files in openspec/changes/ (if the change is still active) or review what you saw during the Step 2 review:

    • Did the spec specify the tax rate? If not, the AI guessed correctly this time, but guessing correctly once is luck, not engineering. A different AI session, a different model, or a different day could produce a different rate.

    • Did the spec specify the rounding strategy? Per item, or on the final total? If not, the AI made a choice. What was it?

    • Did the spec say "applied to the subtotal"? Or could the AI have interpreted "including tax" as tax applied per line item?

      If the receipt does not match your calculation:

      1. Identify the gap. What question did the spec leave unanswered? Tax rate? Rounding? Calculation order?

      2. Check the implementation. What did the AI decide? Look at the backend code or ask the agent: "What tax rate does the receipt calculation use?"

      3. Fix the spec. Open the spec file and add the missing requirement with concrete values. For example:

        ### Requirement: Tax calculation
Tax MUST be calculated at a rate of 8.5%, applied to the order
subtotal (base price plus all customizations). The tax amount
MUST be rounded to the nearest cent using standard rounding
(half-up). Tax MUST appear as a separate line item on the receipt.

      4. Re-apply. Clear context and invoke the apply command to let the agent update the implementation based on the corrected spec.

        This is the core debugging lesson: the bug was in the spec, not the code. The AI implemented exactly what the spec described. If the spec was incomplete, the AI filled the gap with an assumption, and that assumption may or may not match your intent.

  5. Verify the fix. If you made spec changes and re-applied, verify the receipt again with the same test order. The numbers should now match your hand calculation exactly.

    If they still do not match, you have a code bug, not a spec bug. The spec is clear, but the implementation is wrong. Point the agent at the spec and the incorrect code:

    The receipt spec says tax is 8.5% applied to the subtotal, rounded
to the nearest cent. But the receipt shows $0.61 instead of $0.62
for a $7.25 subtotal. Check the tax calculation in the backend and
fix it to match the spec.

  6. Archive. Once the receipt is correct, invoke the archive command.

3.7. Exercise 5: Review the Accumulated System

  1. Examine the spec suite.

    ls openspec/specs/

    Your openspec/specs/ directory now covers four domain areas: drink size selection, order status lifecycle, drink customizations, and order confirmation with receipts. The number of files may exceed four. AI agents often split a domain into separate UI and data specs (for example, drink-customizations and drink-customizations-ui). This is becoming a comprehensive behavioral description of the BeanBotics system.

  2. Examine the archive.

    ls openspec/changes/archive/

    Four archived changes with timestamps. Each one records what changed, why, and how. A complete audit trail of the system’s evolution.

  3. Read a spec. Pick one of the spec files in openspec/specs/ and read through it. Consider:

    • Could a new developer read this spec and understand what the system does without reading the code?

    • Are the scenarios concrete enough to verify?

    • How has the spec suite grown since the first lab?

      The specs are becoming a living document of the system’s behavior. Each lab adds to it. In the next lab, you will use the expanded OpenSpec workflow to build a more complex feature, and the accumulated specs will provide critical context.

3.8. Reflection Questions

Take a few minutes to consider these questions.

  1. How did you adjust your review depth? Compare how you reviewed artifacts in Exercise 2 (quick scan) vs. Exercise 3 (focused review). What made the difference? Was the quick scan appropriate for Change 1, or did you miss something that mattered?

  2. What did you observe during the apply step? Did the agent follow the task order? Did it ask or assume when it hit ambiguity? Did it reference the specs or design? How does your answer affect how much detail you put in future specs?

  3. How did referencing existing specs change Change 2? Did the receipt artifacts reference customization line items? Would they have done so if you had not pointed the agent at openspec/specs/?

  4. What did the tax exercise reveal? Did your spec specify the tax rate and rounding strategy? If not, what did the AI decide? Was the gap obvious before you checked, or did you only notice it when the numbers did not match?

  5. Spec bug vs. code bug: In your debugging experience, was the issue a spec gap (incomplete requirement) or a code bug (correct spec, wrong implementation)? How did you tell the difference?

  6. How would you approach the next feature differently? Based on what you learned about dependencies and spec ambiguity, what would you do differently when proposing a feature that touches pricing or calculations?

3.9. Common Issues

"Menu stuck on 'Loading menu…​' and never populates"

The browser tab is left over from a previous lab. It was open before the current server started, so the menu fetch is pending against a server that no longer exists. Do a hard refresh with Ctrl+Shift+R. If that does not fix it, confirm the URL is http://localhost:8000/ and that uv run uvicorn backend.app:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 is still running in the terminal.

"Customization options not appearing on menu cards"

The server picks up backend changes automatically, but the browser caches frontend files. Do a hard refresh with Ctrl+Shift+R. If that does not help, stop the server with Ctrl+C and restart with uv run uvicorn backend.app:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000.

"Order total does not update when selecting customizations"

Check whether the frontend JavaScript was updated to handle customization price changes. If the spec described dynamic price updates but the implementation does not include them, the task may have been too broad. Ask the agent to look at tasks.md and implement the missing subtask.

"Confirmation step does not appear"

The confirmation step requires frontend changes. If the menu still places orders directly without a review step, the apply may not have completed all tasks. Run openspec validate to check, then re-invoke the apply command if tasks are incomplete.

"Receipt does not show customization line items"

This is likely a dependency issue. Check whether the receipt spec references customizations. If the spec says "display the total" but does not mention individual customization line items, the agent may have shown only a summary. Fix the spec to require itemized customizations, then re-apply.

"Tax amount is off by a cent"

This is the spec ambiguity exercise working as designed. See Exercise 4 for the debugging strategy. The root cause is almost always a missing or imprecise requirement about the tax rate, calculation order, or rounding strategy.

"Archive fails with validation errors"

Run openspec validate to identify structural issues. The most common cause is unchecked tasks in tasks.md. Ensure all tasks are marked complete before archiving.

3.10. Challenge Exercise (Optional)

If you finish early, try one of these exercises.

3.10.1. Manually Add a Tax Scenario

Write a new Given/When/Then scenario for a multi-customization receipt. For example:

#### Scenario: Receipt with multiple customizations
- **Given** a completed order for a Small Machine Learning Mocha ($5.00)
  with extra shot (+$0.75) and whipped cream (+$0.50)
- **When** the customer views the receipt
- **Then** the receipt shows:
  - Small Machine Learning Mocha: $5.00
  - Extra espresso shot: $0.75
  - Whipped cream: $0.50
  - Subtotal: $6.25
  - Tax (8.5%): $0.53
  - Total: $6.78

Add this scenario directly to the relevant spec file in openspec/specs/. This is practice for writing scenarios by hand rather than relying on the AI to generate them. Calculate the tax yourself: $6.25 x 0.085 = $0.53125, rounded to $0.53.

3.10.2. Compare Your Two Cycles

Reflect on the difference between Exercise 2 (fast cycle) and Exercise 3 (thorough cycle):

  • How much time did each take?

  • Did the fast cycle produce any issues that a deeper review would have caught?

  • Where is the line between "fast enough" and "too fast"?

Write down your personal criteria for when to do a quick scan vs. a deep review. You will refine these criteria over the remaining labs.

 Note

Whatever you build in the challenge exercises is for learning purposes. It will not carry forward to the next lab, which starts from a known checkpoint state.

3.11. Context Hygiene Recap

This lab practiced a new context hygiene pattern: clearing between dependent changes.

In the previous lab, you cleared context between exploration and proposing. Different phases of the same feature. In this lab, you cleared context between two different features, then used the archived specs as the bridge.

The pattern:

  1. Build and archive Change 1

  2. Clear context

  3. Point the agent at the relevant specs in openspec/specs/

  4. Propose Change 2 with the dependency made explicit

This works because specs persist in files. When you archive, the behavioral contracts merge into openspec/specs/ and are available to any future session. The agent does not need to remember what it built. It reads the spec.

Compare this to the alternative: keeping context from Change 1 and proposing Change 2 in the same session. That works too. The agent remembers what it just built, but the context window now carries all the noise from Change 1: exploration, failed approaches, cosmetic fixes, debugging side conversations. That noise can degrade the quality of Change 2’s artifacts.

The choice depends on the situation:

  • Clear between changes when Change 1 involved significant debugging or back-and-forth. The noise outweighs the continuity.

  • Keep context when Change 1 was clean and quick, and Change 2 is closely related. The continuity helps.

Use judgment. Context hygiene is about managing signal-to-noise, not following a rigid rule.

3.12. Extra Material: Design Doc Abstraction Level

During lab testing, the AI-generated design.md for the drink customizations change contained implementation details that belong in specs or tasks. Here are the examples that were found and corrected:

Context section. Before:

The Order dataclass stores items: List[str] (a display name like
"Medium Neural Network Latte") and total_price: float (the size
price). The POST /api/orders endpoint accepts
{ "item_id": "...", "size": "..." } only.

Context section. After:

BeanBotics orders currently track a drink name, size, and price.
There is no concept of add-ons or modifiers — every order is the
base drink at the selected size.

Decisions section. Before:

Define CUSTOMIZATIONS: dict[str, float] in models.py mapping option
IDs to prices. This keeps options co-located with the data model
