and avoids a separate data file for three items.

Decisions section. After:

The set of options is small (five items), universal across all
drinks, and unlikely to change frequently. Defining them as a
constant in the backend keeps them co-located with the data model
and avoids a separate data file.

Risks section. Before:

POST /api/orders gains a new field, but it defaults to [],
so existing callers without the field still work.

Risks section. After:

The order API contract changes, but the new field is optional
so existing callers are unaffected.

The principle: Design documents capture decisions and rationale. Why X over Y. Specs define the contract. What the system must do, with testable scenarios. Tasks list implementation steps. Where to make changes. When a design doc includes variable names, type signatures, file paths, exact API bodies, or default values, it is leaking spec-level or task-level detail into the wrong artifact.

 Tip

Your work in ~/LabFiles/implementation-patterns-debugging/beanbotics/ is preserved after the lab. If you want to restart the lab from the clean starting state, re-extract the course materials ZIP (or copy the pristine folder from a fresh extract).

Expanded Workflow & Spec Lifecycle

Lab 4

  • Switch from the core profile to the expanded profile and use the expanded command set
  • Use the new and continue commands to build artifacts incrementally with review gates
  • Apply targeted review questions at each artifact stage to catch issues before they propagate
  • Use the verify command to validate implementation against specifications
  • Identify and fix a spec gap found during verification (customization COGS)
  • Examine how the spec suite evolves through the archive process

4.1. Prerequisites

Before starting this lab, ensure you have:

  • Completed the Implementation Patterns & Debugging lab (or have the checkpoint files)

  • Python 3.10+ with pip

  • Node.js 18+ with npm

  • VS Code (or your preferred editor) with one of the supported AI coding agents installed, signed in, and configured

  • OpenSpec CLI installed (openspec --version to verify)

 Note

Command syntax depends on your agent

Throughout this course, when prose says "invoke the propose command," substitute the command name into the pattern for your agent:

Agent Example: propose

Claude Code

/opsx:propose

Cursor, Windsurf, GitHub Copilot, Google Antigravity

/opsx-propose

Amazon Q, AWS Kiro

@opsx-propose

Apply the same pattern to every other command (apply, archive, continue, …​).

4.2. Introduction

In the previous labs, you used the core workflow: propose, apply, archive. Each time, the propose command generated all four artifacts (proposal, specs, design, and tasks) in a single step. You reviewed the artifacts after they were all generated and made corrections before applying.

That works well for features you can describe in one prompt, but some features have too many interrelated decisions. An ingredient cost model involves ingredient definitions, recipe mappings that vary by drink and size, COGS calculations that must account for customizations, and a live financial dashboard that aggregates across orders. If the proposal captures the wrong scope, the specs, design, and tasks all inherit the mistake. You discover the problem during review, but by then all four artifacts need rework.

This lab introduces the expanded workflow. Instead of generating all artifacts at once, you build them one at a time with review gates between each step. You review the proposal before specs are generated. You review the specs before the design is generated. Each correction compounds. Downstream artifacts benefit from the reviewed upstream artifacts.

The feature: Ingredient cost model with a financial dashboard. Each drink is a recipe that consumes specific quantities of base ingredients. The financial dashboard shows revenue, cost of goods sold (COGS), and gross margin, updating live as orders are placed and completed.

Why this feature needs the expanded workflow: The ingredient cost model involves multiple interrelated decisions. Ingredient definitions, recipe mappings per drink and size, COGS calculations that account for customizations, and a live financial view that aggregates across orders. Before you start, consider: could you write a single proposal prompt that captures all of this with enough precision that the AI gets it right on the first pass?

4.3. Exercise 1: Set Up Your Working Copy

This is the completed state from the previous lab. BeanBotics with size selection, order status lifecycle, drink customizations, and order confirmation with receipts.

  1. Open this lab’s BeanBotics folder in VS Code. The starter state for this lab is at ~/LabFiles/expanded-workflow-spec-lifecycle/beanbotics/. Open it as its own VS Code workspace:

    code ~/LabFiles/expanded-workflow-spec-lifecycle/beanbotics

    If VS Code is already open on a different folder, open a new window (File → New Window) and open this folder in the new window, or close the current window and reopen with the command above.

     Important

    All lab work happens in this VS Code window with beanbotics as the project root. The OpenSpec commands are already available. The checkpoint includes the agent config directories generated by previous labs (each agent reads its own tree; the others are inert).

  2. Set up the Python environment. Open a terminal inside the VS Code window (Ctrl+`) and run:

    uv venv
uv pip install -r requirements.txt

  3. Clean up from the previous lab. If you still have the server running from the previous lab, stop it now. Switch to the terminal where it is running and press Ctrl+C. If you cannot locate that terminal, restart your terminal session to release the port.

  4. Run the app.

    uv run uvicorn backend.app:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000

    Verify the app is running at http://localhost:8000. You should see the full BeanBotics UI: the menu with size selection and customization options, the order confirmation step, the status-grouped order queue, and receipt viewing for completed orders.

  5. Review your starting state. Examine what you are starting with:

    ls openspec/specs/

    You should see seven spec files covering four domain areas from the previous labs: drink size selection, order status lifecycle (two specs), drink customizations (two specs), and order confirmation with receipts (two specs).

    ls openspec/changes/archive/

    Four archived changes with timestamps. The complete history of the BeanBotics system so far.

    Now look at the commands available in your agent’s config directory. The exact path depends on which agent you chose in Lab 1:

Agent Command directory

Claude Code

.claude/commands/opsx/

Cursor

.cursor/commands/

Windsurf

.windsurf/workflows/

GitHub Copilot

.github/prompts/

Google Antigravity

.agent/workflows/

Amazon Q

.amazonq/prompts/

AWS Kiro

.kiro/prompts/

List the contents of your agent’s directory. For example, Claude Code users would run:

+

ls .claude/commands/opsx/

+ You should see the core profile commands: propose, apply, archive, and explore (the file extension varies by agent). In the next exercise, you will switch to the expanded profile, which adds new commands.

4.4. Exercise 2: Switch to the Expanded Profile

Before building artifacts, you need the expanded commands. This exercise switches your OpenSpec profile and explains why.

  1. Read the feature requirements. Before switching profiles, read through the full feature description. Consider whether you could capture all of this in a single propose prompt:

    Ingredient cost model:

    • Define base ingredients with unit costs: espresso ($0.40/shot), milk ($0.05/oz), chocolate ($0.08/oz), whipped cream ($0.25/serving), alternative milk ($0.15/oz)

    • Map each drink and size to ingredient quantities. For example:

      • Neural Network Latte: Small = 1 shot + 8oz milk, Medium = 1 shot + 10oz milk, Large = 2 shots + 12oz milk

      • Machine Learning Mocha: Small = 1 shot + 6oz milk + 2oz chocolate, Medium = 1 shot + 8oz milk + 3oz chocolate, Large = 2 shots + 10oz milk + 4oz chocolate

    • Calculate COGS per order from the recipe: sum of (ingredient quantity x unit cost) for each ingredient

    • Customizations add to COGS too. An extra shot adds the cost of one more espresso shot

      Financial dashboard:

    • Display alongside the order board: total revenue, total COGS, and gross margin (revenue minus COGS)

    • Show a per-order breakdown: order details, revenue (the price the customer paid including tax), COGS, and margin

    • Update live as orders are placed and progress through the lifecycle

      Could you write a single prompt that captures all of this precisely? The ingredient quantities, the COGS calculation including customization costs, the dashboard layout and update behavior? Some of it, probably. All of it with the precision needed to avoid rework? That is harder. This is why the expanded workflow exists.

  2. Switch profiles. In your terminal (not in your agent’s chat panel), run:

    openspec config profile

    You will see the current profile settings and a menu asking what to configure:

    Select Workflows only. This lets you change which actions are available without changing how they are delivered.

    Next, you will see a checkbox list of all available workflows. The core profile has four workflows enabled (Propose change, Explore ideas, Apply tasks, Archive change). Use the arrow keys and Space to toggle on the additional expanded workflows:

    Enable these four additional workflows:

    • New change

    • Continue change

    • Fast-forward

    • Verify change

      Leave Sync specs, Bulk archive, and Onboard unchecked. This lab does not use them. Press Enter to confirm.

      When prompted "Apply changes to this project now?", select Yes. OpenSpec updates the project and regenerates each installed agent’s command files automatically. You do not need to run openspec update separately.

  3. Verify new commands. The new commands should be available in your agent immediately. Verify by checking your agent’s command directory (see the table in Exercise 1 step 5). For example, Claude Code users would run:

    ls .claude/commands/opsx/

    You should now see additional commands alongside the core ones: new, continue, ff, verify, and possibly others (the file extension varies by agent). These are the expanded profile commands you will use throughout this lab.

  4. Understand what changed. The profile switch regenerated your agent’s command files. Your core commands (propose, apply, archive, explore) still work exactly as before. The expanded profile adds options. It does not change the existing workflow.

    In this lab, you will use:

    • new: create an empty change folder (no artifacts generated)

    • continue: generate the next artifact in the dependency chain

    • apply: same as before, implement from the task checklist

    • verify: validate implementation against specs

    • archive: same as before, merge specs and archive

4.5. Exercise 3: Scaffold and Build the Proposal

Now you will begin building the feature using the expanded workflow. Instead of generating all artifacts at once, you start with an empty scaffold and build one artifact at a time.

  1. Scaffold the change. Open your agent’s chat panel and invoke the new command with add-ingredient-cost-model as the change name (see the Command syntax block at the top of this lab for the exact form your agent uses).

    This creates openspec/changes/add-ingredient-cost-model/ with an empty structure. No proposal, no specs, no design, no tasks. You control what gets generated and when.

    Verify the folder was created:

    ls openspec/changes/add-ingredient-cost-model/

  2. Generate the proposal. Now generate the first artifact. The proposal. Provide the feature context so the AI has a clear starting point:

    Read the specs in openspec/specs/ for context on the current system.

This change adds an ingredient cost model and financial dashboard.

Ingredient cost model:
- Define base ingredients with unit costs (espresso per shot,
  milk per oz, chocolate per oz, whipped cream per serving,
  alternative milk per oz)
- Map each drink/size to ingredient quantities — e.g., a Large
  Neural Network Latte uses 2 espresso shots + 12oz milk. Small and
  medium drinks typically use 1 shot; large drinks use 2.
- Calculate COGS per order from the recipe ingredients
- Customization surcharges (like extra shot) must also add their
  ingredient cost to COGS, not just the customer price

Financial dashboard:
- Display alongside the order board showing total revenue,
  total COGS, and gross margin (revenue minus COGS)
- Per-order breakdown showing revenue, COGS, and margin per order
- Updates live as orders are placed and completed

Out of scope: supplier management, ingredient inventory tracking,
purchase ordering, historical reporting, multi-location support.

Then run the continue command. Generate the proposal only and stop --
I will review it before continuing.

    After pasting the context block, invoke the continue command (see the Command syntax block at the top of this lab). The agent should generate the proposal only. Read it before moving on.

     Note

    AI agents sometimes generate more than the proposal in this step (creating specs, design, or tasks alongside it). If that happens, see "`continue` generated more than one artifact" in Common Issues at the end of this lab for guidance on how to recover the review-gate workflow.

  3. Review gate. The proposal. Open openspec/changes/add-ingredient-cost-model/proposal.md and ask these questions:

    • Is the scope right? Are ingredients and recipes treated as one change, or should this be split into two? (One change is correct here. The financial dashboard needs both ingredients and recipes to calculate COGS. Splitting them would create a partial feature.)

    • Are customization costs in scope for COGS? The proposal should explicitly mention that customizations like an extra shot add ingredient cost, not just surcharge revenue. If it does not, add this to the proposal.

    • Is anything in scope that should not be? Watch for scope creep: the AI might add inventory tracking, supplier management, or cost-over-time analytics that you did not ask for. Remove these if present.

      Edit the proposal directly in VS Code if needed. The next artifact (specs) will be generated from this reviewed proposal, so corrections here prevent downstream errors.

       Note

      This is the first review gate. In previous labs, you reviewed all four artifacts after generation. Here, you review one artifact at a time. If the proposal scope is wrong, you fix it before the specs are generated. Rather than discovering the problem in the specs and having to fix both.

4.6. Exercise 4: Build Specs and Design with Review Gates

Continue generating artifacts one at a time. Each invocation of the continue command produces the next artifact in the dependency chain.

  1. Generate the specs. Invoke the continue command in your agent’s chat panel.

    The agent reads the reviewed proposal and generates the delta specs.

  2. Review gate. The specs. Open the generated spec files in openspec/changes/add-ingredient-cost-model/specs/. Ask these questions:

    • Are COGS calculation requirements concrete enough to implement? Look for specific language about how COGS is calculated: sum of (ingredient quantity x unit cost) for each ingredient in the recipe. If the spec just says "calculate COGS" without defining the formula, the AI will guess.

    • Do the specs say how customization costs factor into COGS? An extra espresso shot adds revenue ($0.75 to the customer) _and_ cost ($0.40 for the espresso). The spec should require that COGS includes customization ingredient costs, not just base recipe costs. This is a common gap. Check for it specifically.

    • Does the spec define what "gross margin" means? Revenue minus COGS? As a dollar amount, a percentage, or both? If it is ambiguous, the AI picks one. Make it explicit.

    • Are ingredient quantities specified? The spec should define recipe mappings with concrete numbers (shots, ounces) per drink and size, or describe where they come from (a configuration file, code constants).

      Edit the specs to fill any gaps. In particular, if the customization COGS requirement is missing or vague, add it now. For example:

      ### Requirement: COGS includes customization ingredient costs
COGS for an order MUST include the ingredient cost of any
customizations. For example, an extra espresso shot adds the
cost of one espresso shot to the order's COGS, in addition
to the $0.75 surcharge added to the customer price.

       Tip

      Fixing this gap now is the expanded workflow paying off. If you had used propose, this gap would have propagated into the design and tasks. You would discover it during verification (or worse, in the running application) and have to trace it back through multiple artifacts.

  3. Generate the design. Invoke the continue command again.

    The agent reads the reviewed proposal and reviewed specs to generate the design.

  4. Review gate. The design. Open openspec/changes/add-ingredient-cost-model/design.md and ask:

    • Does the data model handle size-dependent recipes? Large drinks use 2 espresso shots while small and medium use 1. The design should show how recipe mappings vary by size.

    • Where do recipe mappings live? In code as constants? In a configuration file like menu.json? In a separate data file? Any approach works, but the design should make a clear decision. (Code constants or a separate JSON file are both reasonable for this project.)

    • Does the design address how the dashboard updates live? The dashboard shows metrics that change as orders are placed and completed. The design should describe whether this is polling, server-sent events, or recalculated on each page render. (For BeanBotics with its simple in-memory store, polling or recalculating on render is the simplest approach.)

      Edit the design if needed. As with previous labs, watch for implementation details that belong in specs or tasks. Variable names, type signatures, and file paths are signs that the design is at the wrong abstraction level.

  5. Generate the tasks. Invoke the continue command once more.

    The agent reads all reviewed artifacts and generates the task checklist.

  6. Review gate. The tasks. Open openspec/changes/add-ingredient-cost-model/tasks.md and ask:

    • Are tasks ordered by dependency? Ingredient definitions must come before recipe mappings. Recipe mappings must come before COGS calculations. COGS calculations must come before the financial dashboard. If the tasks are out of order, reorder them.

    • Are financial dashboard tasks after the ingredient/recipe tasks they depend on? The dashboard cannot show COGS until the COGS calculation exists.

    • Is each task small enough to verify independently? A task like "implement the full financial dashboard" is too broad. Better: "add COGS calculation endpoint," "add dashboard HTML structure," "wire up live updates."

      Edit the task list if needed. The task order directly controls how apply works through the implementation.

4.7. Exercise 5: Implement and Verify

With all four artifacts reviewed and refined, you are ready to implement. This is the same apply step from previous labs. The expanded workflow changes how you plan, not how you implement.

  1. Clear context and apply. Clear the planning conversation before implementing. The artifacts are finalized in files. The agent reads them fresh. Use your agent’s "clear context" or "new session" affordance (for example, Claude Code users would run /clear).

    Then start implementation by invoking the apply command.

    Watch the agent work through the task checklist. Because you reviewed and refined each artifact before this step, the agent is working from a plan that has been corrected at each stage.

  2. Verify in the browser. Refresh the application at http://localhost:8000 (hard refresh: Ctrl+Shift+R).

    What to check:

    • Financial dashboard visible: You should see a financial panel alongside (or near) the order board showing revenue, COGS, and gross margin. The exact layout depends on the design.

    • Dashboard starts at zero: With no completed orders, revenue, COGS, and margin should all be $0.00 (or the dashboard may show data only after orders are placed. Either is acceptable).

    • Place a test order: Order a Large Neural Network Latte ($6.50) with no customizations. Does the dashboard update? Check whether COGS appears for the order.

      If the dashboard does not appear, hard refresh again. If it still does not appear, check whether all tasks completed. Ask the agent: "Are all tasks in tasks.md checked off?"

  3. Run verify. Now invoke the verify command to check the implementation against the specs.

    The agent reads the specs and checks the code for three things:

    • Completeness: Are all requirements implemented? Are all tasks done?

    • Correctness: Does the implementation match the spec’s intent?

    • Coherence: Do design decisions show up in the code?

      Review the verify output. It may surface issues you did not catch during manual testing. Particularly requirements that were implemented incorrectly or not at all.

  4. The customization COGS test. This is the critical verification. Place a specific test order and check whether the COGS calculation is correct:

    1. Place an order for a Large Neural Network Latte ($6.50) with an extra espresso shot (+$0.75)

    2. Advance the order through the lifecycle to completed

    3. Check the financial dashboard

      Calculate the expected COGS by hand:

      A Large Neural Network Latte recipe uses 2 espresso shots + 12oz milk (based on the ingredient model). The extra shot customization adds 1 more espresso shot:

      Base recipe COGS:
  2 espresso shots x $0.40/shot    = $0.80
  12oz milk x $0.05/oz             = $0.60
  Base COGS                        = $1.40

Customization COGS:
  1 extra espresso shot x $0.40    = $0.40

Total COGS:                        = $1.80

Revenue (pre-tax subtotal):
  Large Neural Network Latte         $6.50
  Extra shot surcharge              + $0.75
  Revenue                           = $7.25

Gross margin: $7.25 - $1.80 = $5.45

       Note

      Your exact ingredient costs and recipe quantities may differ from the example above. Check your spec files in openspec/changes/add-ingredient-cost-model/specs/ for ingredient unit costs, and look at the recipe data file (likely backend/data/ingredients.json or backend/data/recipes.json, depending on your design) for the per-drink, per-size ingredient quantities. Use those values for your hand calculation. The important thing is that the COGS includes the extra shot’s ingredient cost, not just the base recipe cost.

      What to check on the dashboard:

      • Does the COGS for this order include the cost of the extra espresso shot? Or does it only show the base recipe COGS?

      • If COGS is $1.40 (base only) instead of $1.80 (base + customization), the spec gap was not caught. The implementation ignores customization ingredient costs

  5. Fix any issues. If verify or manual testing found problems:

    Spec gap (customization COGS missing): If the COGS calculation ignores customization ingredients, the spec may not have required it explicitly. Open the spec file in openspec/changes/add-ingredient-cost-model/specs/ and add or strengthen the requirement:

    ### Requirement: COGS includes customization ingredient costs
COGS for an order MUST include the ingredient cost of every
customization. An extra espresso shot MUST add the cost of one
espresso shot ($0.40) to the order's COGS. The COGS displayed on
the financial dashboard MUST reflect the full ingredient cost
including customizations.

    Then clear context (using your agent’s clear-context affordance) and invoke the apply command again.

    Code bug (spec is correct but implementation is wrong): If the spec clearly requires customization COGS but the code ignores it, point the agent at the discrepancy:

    The spec requires that COGS includes customization ingredient costs.
The extra shot should add $0.40 to the COGS. Check the COGS
calculation and fix it to include customization ingredients.

    Dashboard not updating: If the numbers are correct but the dashboard does not refresh, this is likely a frontend issue. Ask the agent to check whether the dashboard polls for updates or recalculates on render.

    After fixing, verify again to confirm the fix worked.

4.8. Exercise 6: Archive and Review the Spec Suite

  1. Archive. Once the implementation is verified, invoke the archive command.

  2. Examine the updated spec suite.

    ls openspec/specs/

    The spec suite has grown again. You should see new spec files covering the ingredient cost model and financial dashboard domains alongside the existing specs from previous labs.

    Count the domain areas now covered:

    • Drink size selection

    • Order status lifecycle

    • Drink customizations

    • Order confirmation and receipts

    • Ingredient cost model and financial dashboard (this lab)

      This is becoming a comprehensive behavioral description of the BeanBotics system. Each domain spec reflects the current truth about that part of the system.

  3. Trace the evolution.

    ls openspec/changes/archive/

    Five archived changes with timestamps. Each one records what changed, why, and how. A new developer joining the project could read the spec suite to understand what the system does and browse the archive to understand how it evolved.

    Open one of the earlier archived changes (e.g., the size selection change) and compare its scope to the ingredient cost model change you just completed. Notice how the complexity grew: the first change had a handful of requirements and straightforward scenarios. This change has interrelated requirements across ingredients, recipes, COGS, and the dashboard. The expanded workflow scaled with the complexity.

4.9. Reflection Questions

Take a few minutes to consider these questions.

  1. Which artifact did you change the most during review? Was it the proposal, specs, design, or tasks? What kind of issue did you catch? Would the propose command (all-at-once) have produced the same artifact differently, or would the issue have been there too?

  2. Did catching issues between artifacts save time? Compare the expanded workflow to what would have happened if all four artifacts were generated at once. Would you have caught the same issues during a post-generation review? Would it have been harder to fix them?

  3. What happened with the customization COGS test? Did your spec require it? Did the implementation get it right? If the spec was silent on customization COGS, at what point in the workflow could you have caught it earliest. Proposal, specs, design, or tasks?

  4. When would you use the continue command vs. the propose command? Based on your experience in this lab and previous labs, where is the line? What signals tell you a feature needs incremental artifact generation rather than all-at-once?

  5. How has the spec suite changed? Compare the spec suite now (five domain areas) to where it was after the first feature (one domain area). Is this a document you would hand to a new team member? What would they learn from reading it?

  6. Would you have built this feature differently? If you did this lab again, what would you change about your review at each gate? Were there questions you wish you had asked earlier?

4.10. Common Issues

"Menu stuck on 'Loading menu…​' and never populates"

The browser tab is left over from a previous lab. It was open before the current server started, so the menu fetch is pending against a server that no longer exists. Do a hard refresh with Ctrl+Shift+R. If that does not fix it, confirm the URL is http://localhost:8000/ and that uv run uvicorn backend.app:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 is still running in the terminal.

"New commands not appearing after profile switch"

After running openspec config profile, the new commands should appear in your agent immediately. If they do not, verify the files exist in your agent’s command directory (see the table in Exercise 1 step 5). For example, Claude Code users would run:

ls .claude/commands/opsx/

You should see new, continue, ff, and verify alongside the core commands (the file extension varies by agent). If they are missing, run openspec update to sync the current config into the project.

This especially applies if the TUI showed the expanded workflows already checked and reported "No config changes": that means your global OpenSpec config already has them selected (from a prior install or run), so the TUI skipped regenerating the project files. openspec update is the fix.

If the command files exist in your agent’s command directory but the new commands still do not appear in your agent’s UI, the agent may have cached the command list at startup. Reload your editor window (for VS Code-based agents: Ctrl+Shift+P → Developer: Reload Window; for standalone IDEs like Cursor, Windsurf, or Kiro, restart the application) and the new commands will appear.

"`continue` generated more than one artifact"

The agent should generate one artifact per continue invocation, but sometimes generates several at once. Typically because the agent decided to run the skill repeatedly. Anything beyond proposal.md was created from an un-reviewed proposal, which defeats the review-gate workflow.

To recover:

  1. Review and edit proposal.md first as instructed in Exercise 3.

  2. If your edits substantively changed the proposal, delete the prematurely-generated artifacts (specs/, design.md, tasks.md) inside openspec/changes/add-ingredient-cost-model/ and invoke the continue command again for each one in Exercise 4. The regenerated artifacts will be based on your corrected proposal.

  3. If the proposal needed only minor edits or was already correct, you can keep the existing artifacts and review them at their gates in Exercise 4.

"`continue` generates the wrong artifact"

Continue generates the next artifact based on what is already complete. If it generates specs when you expected the proposal, check whether a proposal already exists in the change folder. Run openspec status --change add-ingredient-cost-model to see which artifacts are done and which are next.

"Financial dashboard does not appear"

Do a hard refresh with Ctrl+Shift+R. If the dashboard still does not appear, check whether all tasks in tasks.md are checked off. The dashboard may be one of the later tasks. Ask the agent: "Are all tasks complete? If not, which ones are remaining?" Then invoke the apply command again.

"COGS shows $0.00 for all orders"

The COGS calculation likely depends on recipe mappings. Check whether the recipe data was created correctly. Ask the agent to check whether the recipe mappings exist and whether the COGS calculation reads them.

"Dashboard does not update after placing orders"

The dashboard may need a page refresh to show updated numbers, depending on the implementation. If the spec requires live updates, check whether the frontend polls the backend or recalculates on demand. A simple fix: ask the agent to add polling that refreshes the dashboard data every few seconds.

"Verify reports issues that seem minor"

The verify command checks comprehensively and may flag issues that are not critical. Use your judgment: if verify says a requirement is not implemented but the behavior is correct (just implemented differently than the spec describes), that may be acceptable. If verify says a requirement is missing entirely, that is worth fixing.

"Archive fails with validation errors"

Run openspec validate to see the specific issues. The most common cause is unchecked tasks in tasks.md. Ensure all tasks are marked complete before archiving.

4.11. Challenge Exercise (Optional)

If you finish early, try one of these exercises.

4.11.1. Calculate Margins for Multiple Orders

Place three different orders with different drinks, sizes, and customizations:

  1. Small Deep Learning Doppio ($3.50), no customizations

  2. Medium Machine Learning Mocha ($6.00), with whipped cream (+$0.50)

  3. Large Neural Network Latte ($6.50), with extra shot ($0.75) and oat milk ($0.60)

Advance all three to completed. Calculate the expected COGS for each by hand using the ingredient costs and recipe mappings in your spec. Compare your hand calculations to the dashboard. Do the numbers match?

This exercise tests whether the COGS calculation handles variation correctly. Different drinks with different recipes, different sizes with different ingredient quantities, and different customizations.

4.11.2. Write a Spec Scenario by Hand

Write a new Given/When/Then scenario for the financial dashboard that is not already in the spec:

#### Scenario: Dashboard reflects cancelled order correctly
- **Given** two orders have been placed with total revenue of $13.75
  and total COGS of $3.50
- **When** one order with revenue $7.25 and COGS $1.80 is cancelled
- **Then** the dashboard SHALL show:
  - Total Revenue: $6.50
  - Total COGS: $1.70
  - Gross Margin: $4.80

Add this scenario to the relevant spec file in openspec/specs/. This is practice for writing scenarios by hand. Does the current implementation handle cancelled orders correctly in the dashboard? If not, you have found a gap.

 Note

Whatever you build in the challenge exercises is for learning purposes. It will not carry forward to the next lab, which starts from a known checkpoint state.

 Tip

Your work in ~/LabFiles/expanded-workflow-spec-lifecycle/beanbotics/ is preserved after the lab. If you want to restart the lab from the clean starting state, re-extract the course materials ZIP (or copy the pristine folder from a fresh extract).

4.12. Context Hygiene Recap

This lab introduced a new context pattern: multiple context sessions within a single feature.

In previous labs, you used one context session for each propose-apply cycle (or cleared between two cycles). In this lab, the expanded workflow naturally involves multiple context interactions:

  • Planning phase: the new command, then multiple continue invocations with review and editing between each

  • Implementation phase: Clear context, then invoke apply with finalized artifacts

  • Verification phase: the verify command followed by any fixes

The key insight: the continue command reads existing artifacts from files, not from conversation history. This means you can clear context between continue steps if the conversation gets cluttered. Each continue step rehydrates from the files. In practice, most developers keep context during the planning phase (the conversation about review decisions is useful) and clear before the apply phase (clean context for implementation).

There is no single right answer. The question to ask is: "Is the context window helping or hurting right now?" If the conversation has accumulated debugging tangents, failed approaches, or off-topic discussions, clear it. If the conversation has useful review decisions that inform the next artifact, keep it.

Real-World Patterns & Spec Management

Lab 5

  • Fix a small issue without using the spec workflow, exercising the judgment call
  • Review the accumulated spec suite and identify which specs contain pricing assumptions
  • Write a proposal and delta specs by hand using ADDED, MODIFIED, and REMOVED sections
  • Have the AI agent review hand-written specs and identify gaps
  • Implement dynamic pricing using the hand-written specs as the starting point
  • Trace the full evolution of the BeanBotics spec suite across the course

5.1. Prerequisites

Before starting this lab, ensure you have:

  • Completed the Expanded Workflow & Spec Lifecycle lab (or have the checkpoint files)

  • Python 3.10+ with pip

  • Node.js 18+ with npm

  • VS Code (or your preferred editor) with one of the supported AI coding agents installed, signed in, and configured (see the Command syntax note below)

  • OpenSpec CLI installed (openspec --version to verify)

 Note

Command syntax depends on your agent

Throughout this course, when prose says "invoke the propose command," substitute the command name into the pattern for your agent:

Agent Example: propose

Claude Code

/opsx:propose

Cursor, Windsurf, GitHub Copilot, Google Antigravity

/opsx-propose

Amazon Q, AWS Kiro

@opsx-propose

Apply the same pattern to every other command (apply, archive, continue, …​).

5.2. Introduction

In every lab so far, you used the spec workflow: propose (or new/continue), apply, archive. Every change introduced new behavior with ADDED sections in the delta specs. The AI generated the artifacts and you reviewed them.

This lab is different in three ways:

  1. You will fix something without SDD. A small cosmetic issue that does not warrant the spec workflow. This is the "when SDD is overkill" judgment call from earlier in the course, practiced for real.

  2. You will write specs by hand. Instead of the AI generating proposals and delta specs, you will write them yourself. The AI becomes your reviewer, not your author.

  3. You will modify existing specs. The dynamic pricing feature changes behavior that has been specified since earlier labs. For the first time, your delta specs will include MODIFIED and REMOVED sections alongside ADDED.

The feature: Dynamic pricing with peak and off-peak tiers. During peak hours (7-9am, 11:30am-1:30pm), drinks are full price. During off-peak hours, base drink prices are 20% off. A time simulation slider in the UI lets you control the current time to test both tiers instantly.

5.3. Exercise 1: Set Up Your Working Copy

In this exercise, you will open this lab’s BeanBotics folder in VS Code, create a virtual environment, install dependencies, and verify the app runs. The starting state is the completed state from the previous lab. BeanBotics with size selection, order lifecycle, customizations, confirmation/receipts, and the ingredient cost model with financial dashboard.

  1. Open this lab’s BeanBotics folder in VS Code. The starter state for this lab is at ~/LabFiles/real-world-patterns-spec-management/beanbotics/. Open it as its own VS Code workspace:

    code ~/LabFiles/real-world-patterns-spec-management/beanbotics

    If VS Code is already open on a different folder, open a new window (File → New Window) and open this folder in the new window, or close the current window and reopen with the command above.

     Important

    For the rest of this lab, all work happens in this VS Code window with beanbotics as the project root. Your agent and the OpenSpec commands only work when VS Code (or your agent’s IDE) is opened to the directory where OpenSpec is initialized. The checkpoint includes expanded profile commands from a previous lab. new, continue, ff, and verify are all available alongside the core commands (see Command syntax above for how these are invoked in your agent).

  2. Set up the Python environment. Open a terminal inside the VS Code window (Ctrl+`) and run:

    uv venv
uv pip install -r requirements.txt

  3. Clean up any previously running server. If you still have a server running from an earlier session, stop it now. Find the terminal where it is running and press Ctrl+C, or restart your terminal session if you cannot locate it.

  4. Run the app.

    uv run uvicorn backend.app:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000

    Verify the app is running at http://localhost:8000. You should see the full BeanBotics UI: menu with size selection and customizations, order confirmation, status-grouped order queue, receipt viewing, and the financial dashboard with revenue/COGS/margin.

  5. Review your starting state.

    ls openspec/specs/

    You should see eleven spec files covering five domain areas built across the earlier labs: drink size selection, order status lifecycle, drink customizations, order confirmation/receipts, and the ingredient cost model with financial dashboard.

    ls openspec/changes/archive/

    Five archived changes with timestamps. The complete history of BeanBotics so far.

5.4. Exercise 2: Fix Without SDD

This exercise is deliberately short. You will find a small cosmetic issue in the app and fix it by talking to the agent directly. No propose command, no specs, no artifacts.

  1. Find a cosmetic issue. Open the app at http://localhost:8000 and look at it with a critical eye. After several labs of AI-generated implementation, there are likely rough edges. Look for things like:

    • Inconsistent spacing or alignment between sections

    • A label or heading that could be clearer

    • Color or font inconsistencies between the menu, order queue, and financial dashboard

    • A button that does not quite match the style of other buttons

    • Missing or unclear column headers in the financial dashboard table

      Pick one small issue. It should be something you can describe in a single sentence.

  2. Fix it directly. Open your agent’s chat panel and describe the issue. No OpenSpec commands. Just tell the agent what to fix:

    The [describe your issue] — fix it to [describe what you want].

    For example: "The financial dashboard table header text is hard to read against the dark background. Make it match the style of the order queue headers."

    The agent fixes it. Done.

  3. Verify and reflect. Refresh the browser (Ctrl+Shift+R) and confirm the fix looks right.

    Now ask yourself: would a spec have helped here?

    • The intent was self-evident. You could describe the fix in one sentence

    • No behavioral requirement changed. The system works the same way

    • No spec in openspec/specs/ needs updating

    • The fix is purely visual

      This is the "when SDD is overkill" heuristic from earlier in the course, practiced for real. The answer is clear: just fix it and move on. Save the spec workflow for behavioral changes.

       Note

      This fix creates spec drift in one sense: the code changed but the specs did not. The specs only cover behavior, not visual styling, and a cosmetic change that does not affect behavior is outside the spec’s scope. No drift has occurred.

5.5. Exercise 3: Identify Pricing Assumptions in the Spec Suite

Before writing delta specs, you need to know what you are modifying. This exercise reviews the accumulated specs to identify where pricing assumptions are baked in.

  1. Read the specs that touch pricing. Open each of these spec files and note where pricing is mentioned:

    cat openspec/specs/drink-size-selection/spec.md
cat openspec/specs/order-receipt/spec.md
cat openspec/specs/financial-dashboard/spec.md

    As you read, note requirements that assume fixed prices. For example:

    • The size selection spec says the menu displays prices per size, but assumes the price is always the base price from menu.json

    • The receipt spec shows itemized pricing with a fixed base price

    • The financial dashboard shows revenue computed from order totals, but does not distinguish between pricing tiers

  2. Identify what changes. For the dynamic pricing feature (full price during peak, 20% off during off-peak), note which existing requirements need to change:

    • Menu display: During off-peak, prices need to show the original price (strikethrough) and the discounted price. During peak, prices show normally.

    • Order placement: The backend needs to calculate the price based on the active pricing tier, not just the static menu price.

    • Receipts: Off-peak orders need a discount line item showing the savings.

    • Financial dashboard: Revenue needs to distinguish peak vs. off-peak to show the impact of discounting.

      Also note what is entirely new (not modifying existing specs):

    • Pricing tier definitions (peak windows, discount percentage)

    • Time simulation slider for testing

    • Backend pricing tier determination

      And what is being removed:

    • The assumption that drink prices are always the fixed base size price from menu.json

      You will use these notes in the next exercise when writing the delta specs.

5.6. Exercise 4: Write Specs by Hand

This is the core exercise of the lab. You will write a proposal and delta specs without AI generation. The AI becomes your reviewer after you finish writing.

  1. Scaffold the change. In your agent’s chat panel, invoke the new command with the argument add-dynamic-pricing (see Command syntax above for the exact form).

    This creates openspec/changes/add-dynamic-pricing/ with an empty structure.

  2. Write the proposal. Open openspec/changes/add-dynamic-pricing/proposal.md in VS Code and write it by hand. Use the structure you have seen across the earlier labs of AI-generated proposals:

    • Why: BeanBotics uses fixed pricing regardless of time of day. Coffee shops typically offer off-peak discounts to attract customers during slow periods. Dynamic pricing lets the business run full prices during peak hours and discount during off-peak.

    • What changes: Pricing tier definitions (peak and off-peak), time simulation for testing, modified menu display, modified order pricing, modified receipts, modified financial dashboard.

    • Scope boundaries: What is out of scope? (Suggestions: per-drink discount overrides, customer-facing tier schedule, historical pricing data, different discount percentages for different times.)

      Keep it skimmable in two minutes.

  3. Write the delta specs. This is the most important step. You will create delta spec files that include ADDED, MODIFIED, and REMOVED sections. The first time you are modifying existing specs rather than only adding new ones.

    Create the Scaffolding

    First, create the folder and file structure for your delta specs. Each domain that this change touches gets its own folder with a spec.md file inside it. This matches the pattern you have seen in every previous lab’s archived changes.

    Create these folders and files inside openspec/changes/add-dynamic-pricing/specs/:

    mkdir -p openspec/changes/add-dynamic-pricing/specs/pricing-tiers
mkdir -p openspec/changes/add-dynamic-pricing/specs/menu-display
mkdir -p openspec/changes/add-dynamic-pricing/specs/order-pricing
mkdir -p openspec/changes/add-dynamic-pricing/specs/order-receipt
mkdir -p openspec/changes/add-dynamic-pricing/specs/financial-dashboard

touch openspec/changes/add-dynamic-pricing/specs/pricing-tiers/spec.md
touch openspec/changes/add-dynamic-pricing/specs/menu-display/spec.md
touch openspec/changes/add-dynamic-pricing/specs/order-pricing/spec.md
touch openspec/changes/add-dynamic-pricing/specs/order-receipt/spec.md
touch openspec/changes/add-dynamic-pricing/specs/financial-dashboard/spec.md

    Your change folder should now look like this:

    openspec/changes/add-dynamic-pricing/
├── proposal.md                          ← written in Step 2
└── specs/
    ├── pricing-tiers/
    │   └── spec.md                      ← ADDED: tier definitions, time slider
    ├── menu-display/
    │   └── spec.md                      ← MODIFIED: strikethrough + discounted price
    ├── order-pricing/
    │   └── spec.md                      ← MODIFIED: tier-based price calculation
    ├── order-receipt/
    │   └── spec.md                      ← MODIFIED: discount line item
    └── financial-dashboard/
        └── spec.md                      ← MODIFIED: peak vs. off-peak breakdown

     Note

    In this lab, we are providing the scaffolding so you can focus on the hard part: writing spec content with ADDED, MODIFIED, and REMOVED sections. In a real project, you would decide this structure yourself. Choosing which domains to create based on which parts of the system the change touches. Look at openspec/specs/ to see the existing domain structure, and create delta spec folders for each domain your change affects. If a change introduces an entirely new domain (like pricing tiers), create a new folder. If it modifies an existing domain (like receipts), create a folder that maps to the existing spec.

    Fill in the Spec Files

    Writing five delta specs from scratch would take longer than the lab allows. Instead, we are providing three of the five delta specs for you and asking you to write the remaining two. This lets you focus your time on the skill this exercise teaches. Writing ADDED, MODIFIED, and REMOVED sections. Without spending the full lab on spec authoring alone.

    In a real project, you would write all of them. The three we provide are realistic examples of what the AI would generate if you invoked continue: study them before writing your own.

    Provided: menu-display/spec.md

    Copy the following into openspec/changes/add-dynamic-pricing/specs/menu-display/spec.md:

    # Delta Spec: Menu Display — Dynamic Pricing

## MODIFIED

### Requirement: Menu cards display size options with prices

**Previous:** Each menu card SHALL display a size selector showing all available sizes (small, medium, large) with their corresponding prices.

**Updated:** Each menu card SHALL display a size selector showing all available sizes (small, medium, large) with pricing that reflects the active pricing tier. During peak hours, prices display normally. During off-peak hours, each size option SHALL show the original price with a strikethrough and the discounted price beside it. A pricing tier indicator SHALL be visible on the menu showing whether peak or off-peak pricing is active.

#### Scenario: Menu during peak hours
- **WHEN** the active time is within a peak window (7:00am-9:00am or 11:30am-1:30pm)
- **THEN** each size option shows its base price normally with no discount indicators
- **AND** the menu displays a "Peak Pricing" indicator

#### Scenario: Menu during off-peak hours
- **WHEN** the active time is outside all peak windows
- **THEN** each size option shows the original base price with a strikethrough and the discounted price (base price minus 20%) beside it
- **AND** the menu displays an "Off-Peak Pricing — 20% Off" indicator

#### Scenario: Prices update when time changes
- **WHEN** the customer adjusts the time simulation slider from a peak time to an off-peak time
- **THEN** the menu prices update immediately to show strikethrough and discounted prices without a page reload
    Provided: order-pricing/spec.md

    Copy the following into openspec/changes/add-dynamic-pricing/specs/order-pricing/spec.md:

    # Delta Spec: Order Pricing — Dynamic Pricing

## MODIFIED

### Requirement: Order is placed with the selected size

**Previous:** When the customer clicks the order button, the system SHALL send the selected size to the API instead of always sending "medium".

**Updated:** When the customer clicks the order button, the system SHALL send the selected size and the current simulated time to the API. The backend SHALL determine the active pricing tier from the simulated time and calculate the base drink price accordingly: full price during peak hours, 20% off during off-peak hours. Customization surcharges (extra shot, milk alternatives, whipped cream) SHALL NOT be discounted — only the base drink price is affected.

#### Scenario: Order placed during peak hours
- **WHEN** the customer places an order for a Large Neural Network Latte ($6.50) at 8:00am (peak)
- **THEN** the backend uses $6.50 as the base price
- **AND** the order total reflects the full base price plus any customization surcharges

#### Scenario: Order placed during off-peak hours
- **WHEN** the customer places an order for a Large Neural Network Latte ($6.50) at 3:00pm (off-peak)
- **THEN** the backend applies a 20% discount to the base price: $6.50 × 0.80 = $5.20
- **AND** the order total reflects $5.20 plus any customization surcharges at full price

#### Scenario: Customization surcharges are not discounted
- **WHEN** the customer places an order at 3:00pm (off-peak) for a Large Neural Network Latte with an extra espresso shot (+$0.75)
- **THEN** the base price is discounted to $5.20 but the extra shot remains $0.75
- **AND** the pre-tax subtotal is $5.95

#### Scenario: Price is locked at order placement time
- **WHEN** the customer places an order at 8:59am (peak) and the time crosses into off-peak (9:01am) before the order is completed
- **THEN** the order retains the peak price from when it was placed

## REMOVED

### Assumption: Drink prices are always the base size price
**Reason:** Drink prices now vary by time of day. The base size price in menu.json represents the full (peak) price. During off-peak hours, a 20% discount is applied to the base drink price. The fixed-price assumption is replaced by tier-based pricing.
    Provided: financial-dashboard/spec.md

    Copy the following into openspec/changes/add-dynamic-pricing/specs/financial-dashboard/spec.md:

    # Delta Spec: Financial Dashboard — Dynamic Pricing

## MODIFIED

### Requirement: Aggregate financial metrics display

**Previous:** The frontend SHALL display a financial dashboard showing three aggregate metrics: total revenue (sum of all order totals), total COGS (sum of all order COGS), and gross margin (revenue minus COGS). The dashboard SHALL appear alongside the order board.

**Updated:** The financial dashboard SHALL display aggregate metrics broken down by pricing tier. The dashboard SHALL show: total revenue, total COGS, and gross margin as before, plus a breakdown showing peak revenue vs. off-peak revenue. This allows the business to see the impact of off-peak discounting on overall revenue and margins.

#### Scenario: Dashboard distinguishes peak and off-peak revenue
- **WHEN** orders have been placed during both peak and off-peak hours
- **THEN** the dashboard displays total revenue with a breakdown showing peak revenue and off-peak revenue separately

#### Scenario: Off-peak margins are lower than peak margins
- **WHEN** the same drink is ordered during peak and off-peak hours
- **THEN** the off-peak order shows lower revenue but the same COGS
- **AND** the off-peak gross margin is lower than the peak gross margin

### Requirement: Per-order financial breakdown

**Previous:** The dashboard SHALL include a per-order breakdown table showing each order's revenue (order total), COGS, and margin (revenue minus COGS).

**Updated:** The per-order breakdown table SHALL include a pricing tier column indicating whether each order was placed during peak or off-peak hours. For off-peak orders, the revenue column reflects the discounted price.

#### Scenario: Per-order row shows pricing tier
- **WHEN** an order was placed during off-peak hours
- **THEN** the per-order row displays "Off-Peak" in the pricing tier column
- **AND** the revenue reflects the discounted base price plus any full-price customization surcharges
    Your Turn: pricing-tiers/spec.md

    Open openspec/changes/add-dynamic-pricing/specs/pricing-tiers/spec.md and write it yourself. This is an ADDED spec. Entirely new requirements, not modifying anything that exists.

    Cover these requirements:

    • Pricing tier definitions: Define the two tiers. Peak windows (7:00am-9:00am and 11:30am-1:30pm) at full price, off-peak (all other hours) at 20% off base drink prices. Specify that customization surcharges are NOT discounted.

    • Time simulation slider: The UI must include a time-of-day slider for selecting the simulated current time. The system uses the slider value to determine the active tier. Menu and pricing must update immediately when the slider changes.

    • Backend time parameter: The API accepts an optional time parameter that overrides server time for pricing tier determination.

      Write Given/When/Then scenarios for at least two of these requirements. Use the ADDED section format and RFC 2119 keywords (MUST, SHALL, MUST NOT) as you have seen in every previous lab.

      Your Turn: order-receipt/spec.md

      Open openspec/changes/add-dynamic-pricing/specs/order-receipt/spec.md and write it yourself. This is a MODIFIED spec. You are changing existing receipt behavior.

      Before writing, read the current receipt spec to see what you are modifying:

      cat openspec/specs/order-receipt/spec.md

      You need to modify the receipt to handle off-peak discounts. Cover:

    • MODIFIED. Receipt line items: Off-peak orders must include an "Off-Peak Discount (20%)" line item showing the discount amount between the base price and the customizations. The subtotal, tax, and total must reflect the discounted price. Peak orders display as they do today (no changes).

      Include the Previous and Updated text for the requirement you are modifying. Quote the previous version from the existing spec in openspec/specs/order-receipt/spec.md. Write at least one Given/When/Then scenario showing a receipt with the discount line item.

       Tip

      Do not worry about getting the specs perfect on your first pass. Write what you can, and the AI will review all five specs in the next step. The three we provided and the two you wrote. The point is to practice writing, not to produce a flawless document.

  4. Have the AI review your specs. This reverses the dynamic from every previous lab. You wrote the specs; now the AI reviews them.

    Read the proposal and delta specs I wrote in
openspec/changes/add-dynamic-pricing/. Compare them
against the existing specs in openspec/specs/.

Review for:
- Gaps: requirements I missed or scenarios I didn't cover
- Ambiguities: language that could be interpreted multiple ways
- Conflicts: new requirements that contradict existing ones
- Edge cases: boundary conditions I didn't address

Tell me what's missing or unclear.

    Read the AI’s feedback carefully. It may identify:

    • Missing scenarios: What happens when a customer is in the confirmation step and the time crosses a tier boundary? (The price should be locked at the time the order is placed, not when it is confirmed.)

    • Rounding ambiguity: How should the 20% discount be rounded? Round the discount amount, or round the final price?

    • Dashboard specifics: How exactly should peak vs. off-peak revenue be displayed? Separate totals? Percentage breakdown?

    • Time simulation defaults: What pricing tier is active when the page first loads? Does the control default to peak or off-peak?

      Edit your specs based on the feedback. You do not need to address every suggestion. Use your judgment about which gaps matter.

5.7. Exercise 5: Implement Dynamic Pricing

With the proposal and specs written and reviewed, generate the remaining artifacts and implement.

  1. Generate design and tasks. You have hand-written the proposal and specs. In your agent’s chat panel, invoke the ff command (see Command syntax above) to generate the design and tasks from them.

    The agent reads your hand-written proposal and specs and generates design.md and tasks.md.

    Review the generated design and tasks briefly. Because your specs are the input, the design and tasks should align with what you wrote. If something looks off, edit it.

  2. Clear context and apply. Clear your agent’s conversation context (for example, with a /clear command or by starting a new session), then invoke the apply command in your agent’s chat panel (see Command syntax above).

    Watch the agent implement from the task list. The specs it follows are the ones you wrote: this is the first time the implementation is driven entirely by your hand-authored specifications.

  3. Verify in the browser. Refresh the application at http://localhost:8000 (hard refresh: Ctrl+Shift+R).

    What to check:

    • Time simulation control visible: You should see a control in the UI for switching between peak and off-peak pricing. The exact UI depends on the implementation. It may be a slider, a toggle, buttons, or a time picker.

    • Peak pricing: Switch to peak pricing. The menu shows full prices with no discount indicators.

    • Off-peak pricing: Switch to off-peak pricing. The menu shows original prices with strikethrough and discounted prices (20% off base drink price).

      Test a specific order in off-peak mode:

      1. Switch to off-peak pricing

      2. Order a Large Neural Network Latte ($6.50 full price, $5.20 with 20% off)

      3. Add an extra shot (+$0.75. NOT discounted)

      4. Confirm and place the order

        Expected receipt values:

        Large Neural Network Latte .......... $6.50
  Off-Peak Discount (20%) ........... -$1.30
  Extra espresso shot ............... $0.75
                          Subtotal    $5.95
                        Tax (8.5%)    $0.51
                             Total    $6.46

         Note

        The discount applies only to the base drink price ($6.50 x 0.20 = $1.30), not to customization surcharges. Tax is calculated on the discounted subtotal. Your exact receipt format may vary depending on how you specified the layout in your delta specs.

        Now test peak mode:

      5. Switch to peak pricing

      6. Place the same order (Large Neural Network Latte + extra shot)

      7. The receipt should show no discount line item. Full price ($6.50 + $0.75 = $7.25 subtotal)

  4. Run verify. Invoke the verify command in your agent’s chat panel (see Command syntax above).

    Because you wrote the specs by hand, verify checks the implementation against your requirements. This is a good test of your spec writing. If verify finds gaps, they may be gaps in your specs, not just the implementation.

  5. Check the financial dashboard. Place a few orders across both pricing tiers (switch between peak and off-peak) and check the financial dashboard:

    • Does the dashboard distinguish peak vs. off-peak revenue?

    • Do the margins differ? (Off-peak revenue is lower but COGS is the same, so margin shrinks)

    • Are the totals correct when you add peak and off-peak orders together?

  6. Fix any issues. If the implementation does not match your specs:

    • Spec gap: Your hand-written spec missed something. Add the requirement and re-apply.

    • Code bug: The spec is clear but the implementation is wrong. Point the agent at the discrepancy.

    • Time simulation not working: Check whether the time control sends the selected time to the backend. Ask the agent to verify the time simulation endpoint.

  7. Archive. Once verified, invoke the archive command in your agent’s chat panel (see Command syntax above).

    This time, archive does more than append. It modifies existing requirements in openspec/specs/ and removes the fixed-price assumption. Check the results:

    cat openspec/specs/drink-size-selection/spec.md

    The spec should now reflect the updated pricing behavior. Requirements you MODIFIED should show the new text. Requirements you REMOVED should be gone.

5.8. Exercise 6: Trace the Full Evolution

  1. The final spec suite.

    ls openspec/specs/

    The spec suite now covers six domain areas built across the course: drink size selection, order status lifecycle, drink customizations, order confirmation with receipts, the ingredient cost model with financial dashboard, and dynamic pricing. The number of spec files may exceed six. AI agents often split a domain into multiple files (for example, separating UI specs from data specs, or COGS calculation from recipe mapping). A new developer joining this project could read this directory and understand every behavioral contract in the system.

  2. The complete archive.

    ls openspec/changes/archive/

    Six archived changes. Each one records the proposal, specs (with ADDED, MODIFIED, or REMOVED sections), design, and tasks. The archive is a complete audit trail of the system’s evolution.

  3. Read a MODIFIED spec. Open the archived dynamic pricing change and look at one of your MODIFIED delta specs. Then open the corresponding file in openspec/specs/ and confirm the modification was applied correctly. The previous text should be gone, replaced by your updated requirement.

    This is the spec lifecycle in action: propose, implement, archive, and the source of truth evolves.

5.9. Reflection Questions

Take a few minutes to consider these questions.

  1. How did it feel to fix something without SDD? Was the judgment call obvious? Would you have hesitated in a real project, or is the line clear between "just fix it" and "use the spec workflow"?

  2. What was hardest about writing specs by hand? Was it the MODIFIED sections (quoting the previous version), the scenarios (concrete values), or the scope boundaries (what to include vs. leave out)? How did writing compare to reviewing AI-generated specs?

  3. What did the AI’s review of your specs catch? Were the gaps it found things you would have caught during implementation anyway, or were they genuinely non-obvious?

  4. How did MODIFIED and REMOVED feel in practice? Was it clear which existing requirements needed to change? Did you have trouble deciding what to put in REMOVED vs. MODIFIED?

  5. Look at the final spec suite. Could a new developer read it and understand the system? What would they learn that the code alone does not communicate? What would still be unclear?

  6. What would you do differently next time? If you could redo this lab, would you write the specs differently? Would you ask the AI for more or less help?

5.10. Common Issues

"Menu stuck on 'Loading menu…​' and never populates"

The browser tab is left over from a previous lab. It was open before the current server started, so the menu fetch is pending against a server that no longer exists. Do a hard refresh with Ctrl+Shift+R. If that does not fix it, confirm the URL is http://localhost:8000/ and that uv run uvicorn backend.app:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 is still running in the terminal.

"Time simulation control does not appear"

Do a hard refresh with Ctrl+Shift+R. If the control still does not appear, check whether all tasks in tasks.md are checked off. The time simulation may be one of the later tasks. Re-invoke apply if tasks are incomplete.

"Prices do not change when switching between peak and off-peak"

Check whether the time control is sending the selected time to the backend. Open the browser developer tools (F12) and watch the network tab as you switch pricing tiers. You should see requests being made. If no requests are sent, the frontend is not wired up to the control. Ask the agent to check the event handler.

"Off-peak discount is applied to customization surcharges"

Your spec should state that customization surcharges are not discounted. If it does and the implementation discounts them anyway, this is a code bug. Point the agent at the spec. If your spec did not specify this, it is a spec gap. Add the requirement and re-apply.

"Receipt does not show discount line item"

Check your MODIFIED spec for the receipt. Did you specify that off-peak orders must include an "Off-Peak Discount" line item? If the spec is there but the receipt does not show it, the implementation missed a requirement. Invoke verify to confirm, then ask the agent to fix it.

"Financial dashboard does not distinguish peak vs. off-peak"

This may not have been implemented if your spec was not specific enough about how the distinction should appear. Check your MODIFIED spec for the financial dashboard. If it just says "distinguish peak and off-peak" without specifying how (separate columns, separate rows, percentage breakdown), the AI may have interpreted it minimally.

"Archive changes existing specs incorrectly"

Run openspec validate before archiving. If validation passes but the merged specs look wrong after archive, check your MODIFIED sections. The previous text must match what is currently in openspec/specs/ for the merge to work correctly.

"The ff command regenerates the proposal and specs I wrote"

If ff overwrites your hand-written files, the change folder may not have recognized them as complete. Check that your proposal and spec files are saved and non-empty. If needed, invoke continue instead to generate only the design, then invoke continue again for tasks.

5.11. Challenge Exercise (Optional)

If you finish early, try one of these exercises.

5.11.1. Add a Third Pricing Tier

Write a delta spec by hand for a "late night" pricing tier (after 8pm) with a 30% discount. This exercises writing MODIFIED sections for requirements you just modified. The pricing tier definitions need to be updated from two tiers to three.

Consider: does this change require MODIFIED sections for the menu display, receipt, and dashboard specs you just wrote? Or are those specs already general enough to handle a third tier without modification?

5.11.2. Calculate the Margin Impact

Place three orders across pricing tiers:

  1. Large Neural Network Latte during peak (full price $6.50)

  2. Large Neural Network Latte during off-peak ($5.20)

  3. Large Neural Network Latte during peak again (full price $6.50)

All three have the same COGS (same recipe, same ingredients). Calculate the gross margin for each by hand. How much does the off-peak discount reduce the margin?

This is the financial reality of discounting. Revenue drops but costs stay the same. The financial dashboard should make this visible.

 Note

Whatever you build in the challenge exercises is for learning purposes. It will not carry forward. This is the final lab.

 Tip

Your work in ~/LabFiles/real-world-patterns-spec-management/beanbotics/ is preserved after the lab. If you want to restart the lab from the clean starting state, re-extract the course materials ZIP (or copy the pristine folder from a fresh extract).

5.12. Context Hygiene Recap

This lab used several context patterns from across the course:

  • No context needed (Exercise 2). The cosmetic fix was small enough that a single conversational message handled it

  • Clean context for spec review (Exercise 4). The AI reviewed your hand-written specs in a focused session

  • Clean context for implementation (Exercise 5). Cleared before apply so the agent reads finalized artifacts fresh

The final pattern is worth reflecting on: specs are persistent context that survives any context window. Conversations disappear when you close the session. Specs persist in files. The spec suite you built across the course is the system’s memory. It captures intent, decisions, constraints, and behavioral contracts that no chat history could preserve.

When you return to a project after a break, you do not need to reconstruct what you were thinking. You read the specs. When a new team member joins, they do not need to interview everyone who worked on the project. They read the specs. When an AI agent starts a fresh session with no conversation history, it reads the specs and knows what the system does.

This is the payoff of Spec-Driven Development: durable, structured context that outlives any single session.
